Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *Find sources:* ["Rugby union"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Rugby+union%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Rugby+union%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Rugby+union%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Rugby+union%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Rugby+union%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Rugby+union%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(August 2025)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))*

* [Rugby ball](/wiki/Football_(ball)#Rugby_union "Football (ball)") * [Scrum cap](/wiki/Scrum_cap "Scrum cap") (optional) * [Rugby boots](/wiki/Cleat_(shoe) "Cleat (shoe)") * [Mouthguard](/wiki/Mouthguard "Mouthguard") * Kicking tee (optional) (often used by kickers) * Shoulder pads (optional)

Worldwide (most popular in certain European and [Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") countries, in [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"), and in [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina"))

Part of the Summer Olympic programme in [1900](/wiki/1900_Summer_Olympics "1900 Summer Olympics"), [1908](/wiki/1908_Summer_Olympics "1908 Summer Olympics"), [1920](/wiki/1920_Summer_Olympics "1920 Summer Olympics") and [1924](/wiki/1924_Summer_Olympics "1924 Summer Olympics") [Rugby sevens](/wiki/Rugby_sevens "Rugby sevens") included since [2016](/wiki/2016_Summer_Olympics "2016 Summer Olympics")

[newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Rugby+union%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Rugby+union%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Rugby+union%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Rugby+union%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(August 2025)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

or often just **rugby**, is a [close-contact](/wiki/Contact_sport#Terminology "Contact sport") [team sport](/wiki/Team_sport "Team sport") that originated at [Rugby School](/wiki/Rugby_School "Rugby School") in [England](/wiki/England "England") in the first half of the 19th century. Rugby involves running with the ball in hand. In its most common form, the game is played between two teams of 15 players each, using an [oval-shaped ball](/wiki/Rugby_ball "Rugby ball") on a rectangular field called a pitch. The field has H-shaped [goalposts](/wiki/Goal_(sports)#Structure "Goal (sports)") at both ends.

**Rugby union football**, commonly known simply as **rugby union** or often just **rugby**, is a [close-contact](/wiki/Contact_sport#Terminology "Contact sport") [team sport](/wiki/Team_sport "Team sport") that originated at [Rugby School](/wiki/Rugby_School "Rugby School") in [England](/wiki/England "England") in the first half of the 19th century. Rugby involves running with the ball in hand. In its most common form, the game is played between two teams of 15 players each, using an [oval-shaped ball](/wiki/Rugby_ball "Rugby ball") on a rectangular field called a pitch. The field has H-shaped [goalposts](/wiki/Goal_(sports)#Structure "Goal (sports)") at both ends.

Rugby union is a popular sport around the world, played by people regardless of gender, age or size.[[3]](#cite_note-4) In 2023, there were more than 10 million players worldwide, of whom 8.4 million were registered. [World Rugby](/wiki/World_Rugby "World Rugby"), previously called the International Rugby Football Board (IRFB) and the International Rugby Board (IRB), has been the governing body for rugby union since 1886, and currently has 116 countries as full members and 18 associate members.

In 1845, the first rules of the game were written by pupils at Rugby School; other significant events in the early development of rugby include the decision by [Blackheath F.C.](/wiki/Blackheath_F.C. "Blackheath F.C.") to leave [The Football Association](/wiki/The_Football_Association "The Football Association") in 1863 and, in 1895, the [split](/wiki/History_of_rugby_league#The_schism_in_England "History of rugby league") between rugby union and [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league"). Historically rugby union was an amateur sport, but in 1995 formal restrictions on payments to players were removed, making the game open to professionals at the highest level for the first time.[[4]](#cite_note-5)

Rugby union spread from the [Home Nations](/wiki/Home_Nations "Home Nations") of the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") and [Ireland](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland "Republic of Ireland"), with other early proponents of the sport including [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand"), [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa") and [France](/wiki/France "France"). The sport is primarily followed in the United Kingdom, Ireland, France, New Zealand, Australia, [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"), [Fiji](/wiki/Fiji "Fiji"), [Tonga](/wiki/Tonga "Tonga"), [Samoa](/wiki/Samoa "Samoa"), [Georgia](/wiki/Georgia_(country) "Georgia (country)"), [Southern Africa](/wiki/Southern_Africa "Southern Africa"), [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina"), and in more recent times, [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"), [Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea"), [South America](/wiki/South_America "South America"), [the United States](/wiki/The_United_States "The United States") and [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"),[[5]](#cite_note-urlU.S_Rugby_Scholarships_-_U.S._Sports_Scholarships-6)[[6]](#cite_note-urlRugby:_Fastest_growing_sport_in_the_U.S._also_one_of_the_oldest_-_Global_Sport_Matters-7)[[7]](#cite_note-urlWhere_Is_Rugby_the_Most_Popular_Among_Students:_Comparison_of_US_and_UK_Student_Leagues_{{!}}_Love_Rugby_League-8) its growth occurring during the expansion of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") and through French proponents ([Rugby Europe](/wiki/Rugby_Europe "Rugby Europe")) in Europe. Countries that have adopted rugby union as their *[de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto")* [national sport](/wiki/National_sport "National sport") include [Fiji](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Fiji "Rugby union in Fiji"), [Georgia](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Georgia "Rugby union in Georgia"), [Madagascar](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Madagascar "Rugby union in Madagascar"),[[8]](#cite_note-9) [New Zealand](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_New_Zealand "Rugby union in New Zealand"), [Samoa](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Samoa "Rugby union in Samoa"), [Tonga](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Tonga "Rugby union in Tonga"), and [Wales](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Wales "Rugby union in Wales").

International matches have taken place since 1871 when the first game was played between Scotland and England at [Raeburn Place](/wiki/Raeburn_Place "Raeburn Place") in [Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh "Edinburgh"). The [Rugby World Cup](/wiki/Rugby_World_Cup "Rugby World Cup"), first held in 1987, is held every four years. The [Six Nations Championship](/wiki/Six_Nations_Championship "Six Nations Championship") in Europe and [The Rugby Championship](/wiki/The_Rugby_Championship "The Rugby Championship") in the Southern Hemisphere are other important international competitions that are held annually.

The [Women's Rugby World Cup](/wiki/Women%27s_Rugby_World_Cup "Women's Rugby World Cup"), first held in 1991, and the [United States](/wiki/United_States_women%27s_national_rugby_union_team "United States women's national rugby union team") won their inaugural title. There is also the [Women's Six Nations Championship](/wiki/Women%27s_Six_Nations_Championship "Women's Six Nations Championship") which is held annually alongside the men's counterpart.

National club and provincial competitions include the [Premiership](/wiki/Premiership_Rugby "Premiership Rugby") in England, the [Top 14](/wiki/Top_14 "Top 14") in France, the [Bunnings NPC](/wiki/National_Provincial_Championship_(2006%E2%80%93present) "National Provincial Championship (2006–present)") in New Zealand, the [League One](/wiki/Japan_Rugby_League_One "Japan Rugby League One") in Japan and the [Currie Cup](/wiki/Currie_Cup "Currie Cup") in South Africa. Other transnational club competitions include the [United Rugby Championship](/wiki/United_Rugby_Championship "United Rugby Championship") of club teams from Ireland, Italy, Scotland, South Africa and Wales, [European Rugby Champions Cup](/wiki/European_Rugby_Champions_Cup "European Rugby Champions Cup") in Europe, and [Super Rugby](/wiki/Super_Rugby "Super Rugby") in Australia, New Zealand and the Pacific Islands.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_union&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main articles: [Rugby football](/wiki/Rugby_football "Rugby football") and [History of rugby union](/wiki/History_of_rugby_union "History of rugby union")

Primitive forms of football included [harpastum](/wiki/Harpastum "Harpastum"), played by the Romans, where two opposing teams competed to throw a large ball into an opposition net, fighting for possession of the ball. Medieval Irishmen played [caid](/wiki/Caid_(sport) "Caid (sport)"), carrying an inflated bladder and using arched trees as goals. The Welsh played an inter-parish game using a wooden ball called [cnapan](/wiki/Cnapan "Cnapan"). Frenchmen played *[la soule](/wiki/La_soule "La soule")* and Georgians played *[lelo burti](/wiki/Lelo_burti "Lelo burti")*. "Football" as a game was well established by 1803 at [Rugby School](/wiki/Rugby_School "Rugby School") and by the 1830s, "running in" with the ball in hand was allowed but subject to hacking and "collaring".[[9]](#cite_note-10)

[![logo](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Wikisource-logo.svg/40px-Wikisource-logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Wikisource-logo.svg)

English [Wikisource](/wiki/Wikisource "Wikisource") has original text related to this article:

**[Laws of Football as played at Rugby School](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/en:Laws_of_Football_as_played_at_Rugby_School "s:en:Laws of Football as played at Rugby School")**

[![A wide shot of an old English school with a central tower, with a sports pitch in the foreground.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Rugby_School_850.jpg/250px-Rugby_School_850.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rugby_School_850.jpg)

Rugby School in Rugby, Warwickshire, with a rugby football pitch in the foreground

Former Rugby School student [Albert Pell](/wiki/Albert_Pell "Albert Pell") is credited with having formed the first "football" team while a student at [Cambridge University](/wiki/Cambridge_University "Cambridge University").[[10]](#cite_note-Marshall13-11) Major private schools each used different rules during this early period, with former pupils from Rugby and [Eton](/wiki/Eton_College "Eton College") attempting to carry their preferred rules through to their universities.[[11]](#cite_note-Marshall13-14-12) A significant event in the early development of rugby football was the production of a written set of rules at Rugby School in 1845,[[12]](#cite_note-13)[[13]](#cite_note-14) followed by the [Cambridge Rules](/wiki/Cambridge_Rules "Cambridge Rules") that were drawn up in 1848.[[14]](#cite_note-15)

Formed in 1863, the national governing body [The Football Association](/wiki/The_Football_Association "The Football Association") (FA) began codifying a set of universal football rules. These new rules specifically banned players from running with the ball in hand and also disallowed [hacking](/wiki/Hacking_(rugby) "Hacking (rugby)") (kicking players in the shins), both of which were legal and common tactics under the Rugby School's rules of the sport. In protest at the imposition of the new rules, the [Blackheath Club](/wiki/Blackheath_F.C. "Blackheath F.C.") left the FA[[15]](#cite_note-Godwin10-16)[[16]](#cite_note-17) followed by several other clubs that also favoured the "Rugby Rules". Although these clubs decided to ban hacking soon afterwards, the split was permanent, and [the FA's codified rules](/wiki/Laws_of_the_Game_(association_football) "Laws of the Game (association football)") became known as "[association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football")" whilst the clubs that had favoured the Rugby Rules formed the [Rugby Football Union](/wiki/Rugby_Football_Union "Rugby Football Union") in 1871,[[15]](#cite_note-Godwin10-16) and their code became known as "[rugby football](/wiki/Rugby_football "Rugby football")".

In 1895, there was [a major schism within rugby football](/wiki/History_of_rugby_league#The_schism_in_England "History of rugby league") in England in which numerous clubs from Northern England resigned from the RFU over the issue of reimbursing players for time lost from their workplaces. The split highlighted the social and class divisions in the sport in England. Although the rules of the game were not a factor in the split, the breakaway teams subsequently adopted some rule changes and this became the separate code of "[rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league")". The RFU's code thereafter took on the name "rugby union" to differentiate it from rugby league,[[17]](#cite_note-18) but both versions of the sport are known simply as "rugby" throughout most of the world.[[18]](#cite_note-19)

### First internationals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_union&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: First internationals")]

The [first rugby football international](/wiki/1871_England_versus_Scotland_rugby_union_match "1871 England versus Scotland rugby union match") was played on 27 March 1871 between [Scotland](/wiki/Scottish_Rugby_Union "Scottish Rugby Union") and [England](/wiki/England_national_rugby_union_team "England national rugby union team") in [Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh "Edinburgh"). Scotland won the game by one goal and one try to one goal.[[15]](#cite_note-Godwin10-16)[[19]](#cite_note-20) By 1881 both [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland") and [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales") had representative teams and in 1883 the first international competition, the [Home Nations Championship](/wiki/Six_Nations_Championship "Six Nations Championship") had begun. 1883 is also the year of the first [rugby sevens](/wiki/Rugby_sevens "Rugby sevens") tournament, the [Melrose Sevens](/wiki/Melrose_Sevens "Melrose Sevens"),[[20]](#cite_note-Godwin12-21) which is still held annually.

Two important overseas tours took place in 1888: a [British Isles](/wiki/1888_British_Lions_tour_to_New_Zealand_and_Australia "1888 British Lions tour to New Zealand and Australia") team visited Australia and New Zealand—although a private venture, it laid the foundations for future [British and Irish Lions](/wiki/British_and_Irish_Lions "British and Irish Lions") tours;[[21]](#cite_note-22) and the [1888–89 New Zealand Native football team](/wiki/1888%E2%80%9389_New_Zealand_Native_football_team "1888–89 New Zealand Native football team") brought the first overseas team to British spectators.[[22]](#cite_note-23)

[![A black and white photo of a rugby field in which three men in military uniform, one of whom is King George V, present a silver trophy to a rugby player dressed in black kit. Behind in a line are the rest of the team.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/King_George_V_presents_a_cup_to_the_captain_of_the_winning_New_Zealand_Services_Rugby_Team%2C_London.jpeg/250px-King_George_V_presents_a_cup_to_the_captain_of_the_winning_New_Zealand_Services_Rugby_Team%2C_London.jpeg)](/wiki/File:King_George_V_presents_a_cup_to_the_captain_of_the_winning_New_Zealand_Services_Rugby_Team,_London.jpeg)

[James Ryan](/wiki/James_Ryan_(rugby_union_player_born_1887) "James Ryan (rugby union player born 1887)"), captain of the New Zealand Army team, receiving the Kings Cup from George V

During the early history of rugby union, a time before commercial air travel, teams from different continents rarely met. The first two notable tours both took place in 1888—the British Isles team touring New Zealand and Australia,[[23]](#cite_note-Lions_history-24) followed by the New Zealand team touring Europe.[[24]](#cite_note-25) Traditionally the most prestigious tours were the Southern Hemisphere countries of Australia, New Zealand and South Africa making a tour of a Northern Hemisphere, and the return tours made by a joint British and Irish team.[[25]](#cite_note-26) Tours would last for months, due to long traveling times and the number of games undertaken; the 1888 New Zealand team began their tour in Hawkes Bay in June and did not complete their schedule until August 1889, having played 107 rugby matches.[[26]](#cite_note-27) Touring international sides would play test matches against international opponents, including national, club and county sides in the case of Northern Hemisphere rugby, or provincial/state sides in the case of Southern Hemisphere rugby.[[23]](#cite_note-Lions_history-24)[[27]](#cite_note-28)

Between 1905 and 1908, all three major Southern Hemisphere rugby countries sent their first touring teams to the Northern Hemisphere: [New Zealand](/wiki/The_Original_All_Blacks "The Original All Blacks") in 1905, followed by [South Africa](/wiki/1906%E2%80%9307_South_Africa_rugby_union_tour "1906–07 South Africa rugby union tour") in 1906 and [Australia](/wiki/1908%E2%80%9309_Australia_rugby_union_tour_of_Britain "1908–09 Australia rugby union tour of Britain") in 1908. All three teams brought new styles of play, fitness levels and tactics,[[28]](#cite_note-Godwin,_p18-29) and were far more successful than critics had expected.[[29]](#cite_note-30)

The New Zealand 1905 touring team performed a [haka](/wiki/Ka_Mate "Ka Mate") before each match, leading Welsh Rugby Union administrator [Tom Williams](/wiki/Tom_Williams_(rugby_union,_born_1859) "Tom Williams (rugby union, born 1859)") to suggest that Wales player [Teddy Morgan](/wiki/Teddy_Morgan "Teddy Morgan") lead the crowd in singing the Welsh national anthem, *[Hen Wlad Fy Nhadau](/wiki/Hen_Wlad_Fy_Nhadau "Hen Wlad Fy Nhadau")*, as a response. After Morgan began singing, the crowd joined in: the first time a national anthem was sung at the start of a sporting event.[[30]](#cite_note-31)[[nb 2]](#cite_note-33) In 1905 France played England in its first international match.[[28]](#cite_note-Godwin,_p18-29)

Rugby union was included as an event in the Olympic Games four times during the early 20th century. No international rugby games and union-sponsored club matches were played during the First World War, but competitions continued through service teams such as the [New Zealand Army team](/wiki/New_Zealand_Army_rugby_team_of_1919 "New Zealand Army rugby team of 1919").[[32]](#cite_note-34) During the Second World War no international matches were played by most countries, though Italy, Germany and Romania played a limited number of games,[[33]](#cite_note-35)[[34]](#cite_note-36)[[35]](#cite_note-37) and [Cambridge](/wiki/Cambridge_University_R.U.F.C. "Cambridge University R.U.F.C.") and [Oxford](/wiki/Oxford_University_RFC "Oxford University RFC") continued their annual [University Match](/wiki/The_Varsity_Match "The Varsity Match").[[36]](#cite_note-38)

The first officially sanctioned international rugby sevens tournament took place in 1973 at Murrayfield, one of Scotland's biggest stadiums, as part of the [Scottish Rugby Union](/wiki/Scottish_Rugby_Union "Scottish Rugby Union") centenary celebrations.[[37]](#cite_note-39)

### World Cup and professionalism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_union&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: World Cup and professionalism")]

In 1987, the first Rugby World Cup was held in Australia and New Zealand, and the inaugural winners were New Zealand. The first World Cup Sevens tournament was held at Murrayfield in 1993. Rugby Sevens was introduced into the [Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Commonwealth_Rugby_Sevens_Championships "Commonwealth Rugby Sevens Championships") in 1998 and was added to the Olympic Games of 2016.[[38]](#cite_note-I.O.C._Decision-40) Both men and women's Sevens took place at the 2020 Olympic Games in Tokyo.[[39]](#cite_note-Tokyo2020.org-41)

Rugby union was an amateur sport until the IRB declared the game "open" in August 1995 (shortly after the completion of the 1995 World Cup), removing restrictions on payments to players.[[40]](#cite_note-Stubbs3-42)[[41]](#cite_note-43) However, the pre-1995 period of rugby union was marked by frequent accusations of "[shamateurism](/wiki/Shamateurism "Shamateurism")",[[42]](#cite_note-44) including an investigation in Britain by a House of Commons Select committee in early 1995.[[43]](#cite_note-45)[[44]](#cite_note-46) Following the introduction of professionalism trans-national club competitions were started, with the [Heineken Cup](/wiki/Heineken_Cup "Heineken Cup") in the Northern Hemisphere and [Super Rugby](/wiki/Super_Rugby "Super Rugby") in the Southern Hemisphere.[[45]](#cite_note-historymain-47)[[46]](#cite_note-Gaynor-48)

The [Tri Nations](/wiki/The_Rugby_Championship "The Rugby Championship"), an annual international tournament involving Australia, New Zealand and South Africa, kicked off in 1996.[[46]](#cite_note-Gaynor-48) In [2012](/wiki/2012_Rugby_Championship "2012 Rugby Championship"), this competition was extended to include [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina_national_rugby_union_team "Argentina national rugby union team"), a country whose impressive performances in international games (especially finishing in third place in the 2007 Rugby World Cup) was deemed to merit inclusion in the competition. As a result of the expansion to four teams, the tournament was renamed The Rugby Championship.[[47]](#cite_note-The_Rugby_Championship_to_replace_Tri_Nations-49)

## Teams and positions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_union&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Teams and positions")]

Main article: [Rugby union positions](/wiki/Rugby_union_positions "Rugby union positions")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Rugby_Union_Formation.svg/250px-Rugby_Union_Formation.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Rugby_Union_Formation.svg)

A standard rugby union team formation illustrating each of the positions and their respective numbers

Each team starts the match with 15 players on the field and typically seven or eight substitutes but this varies at amateur level.[[48]](#cite_note-Law3-50) The 15 players on the field are divided into eight [forwards](/wiki/Rugby_union_positions#Forwards "Rugby union positions") (two more than in [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league")) and seven [backs](/wiki/Rugby_union_positions#Backs "Rugby union positions").[[49]](#cite_note-IRB_BGRU_6-51) There are typically significantly more forwards on the bench than backs with, for example, South Africa having a 7–1 split in favour of forwards in their August 2023 match against New Zealand in Twickenham. A 6-2 or 5–3 split is more common.

### Forwards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_union&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Forwards")]

The main responsibilities of the forward players are to gain and retain possession of the ball. Forwards play a vital role in tackling and rucking opposing players.[[50]](#cite_note-talkrugby-52) Players in these positions are generally bigger and stronger and take part in the [scrum](/wiki/Scrum_(rugby) "Scrum (rugby)") and line-out.[[50]](#cite_note-talkrugby-52) The forwards are often collectively referred to as the 'pack', especially when in the scrum formation.[[51]](#cite_note-Glossary-53)

#### Front row

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_union&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Front row")]

The front row consists of three players: two [props](/wiki/Rugby_union_positions#Prop "Rugby union positions") (the loosehead prop and the tighthead prop) and the [hooker](/wiki/Rugby_union_positions#Hooker "Rugby union positions"). The role of the two props is to support the hooker during scrums, to provide support for the jumpers during line-outs and to provide strength and power in rucks and mauls. The third position in the front row is the hooker. The hooker is a key position in attacking and defensive play and is responsible for winning the ball in the scrum. Hookers normally throw the ball in at line-outs.[[49]](#cite_note-IRB_BGRU_6-51)[[52]](#cite_note-54)

#### Second row

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_union&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Second row")]

The second row consists of two [locks](/wiki/Rugby_union_positions#Locks "Rugby union positions") or lock forwards. Locks are usually the tallest players in the team and specialise as line-out jumpers.[[49]](#cite_note-IRB_BGRU_6-51) The main role of the lock in line-outs is to make a standing jump, often supported by the other forwards, to either collect the thrown ball or ensure the ball comes down on their side. Locks also have an important role in the scrum, binding directly behind the three front row players and providing forward drive.[[49]](#cite_note-IRB_BGRU_6-51)

[![Facing right a group of seven men, in blue and white hooped jerseys, bind together and crouch to form a scrum. The eighth player stands behind them observing the off-picture opposition.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Chabal_Rugby_Racing_vs_Stade_Toulousain_311009.jpg/250px-Chabal_Rugby_Racing_vs_Stade_Toulousain_311009.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chabal_Rugby_Racing_vs_Stade_Toulousain_311009.jpg)

[Sébastien Chabal](/wiki/S%C3%A9bastien_Chabal "Sébastien Chabal") (far left) in number eight position before  
entering the scrum

#### Back row

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_union&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Back row")]

The back row, not to be confused with 'Backs', is the third and final row of the forward positions, who are often referred to as the loose forwards.[[51]](#cite_note-Glossary-53) The three positions in the back row are the two [flankers](/wiki/Rugby_union_positions#Flanker "Rugby union positions") and the [number 8](/wiki/Rugby_union_positions#Number_eight "Rugby union positions"). The two flanker positions, known as the blindside flanker and openside flanker, are the final row in the scrum. They are usually the most mobile forwards in the game. Their main role is to win possession through 'turn overs'.[[49]](#cite_note-IRB_BGRU_6-51) The number 8 packs down between the two locks at the back of the scrum. The role of the number 8 in the scrum is to control the ball after it has been heeled back from the front of the pack, and the position provides a link between the forwards and backs during attacking phases.[[53]](#cite_note-IRB_BGRU_7-55)

### Backs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_union&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Backs")]

The role of the backs is to create and convert point-scoring opportunities. They are generally smaller, faster and more agile than the forwards.[[50]](#cite_note-talkrugby-52) Another distinction between the backs and the forwards is that the backs are expected to have superior kicking and ball-handling skills, especially the fly-half, scrum-half, and full-back.[[50]](#cite_note-talkrugby-52)

#### Half-backs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_union&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Half-backs")]

The half-backs consist of two positions, the [scrum-half](/wiki/Rugby_union_positions#Scrum-half "Rugby union positions") and the [fly-half](/wiki/Rugby_union_positions#Fly-half "Rugby union positions"), also known in the Southern Hemisphere as half-back and first five-eighth respectively. The fly-half is crucial to a team's game plan, orchestrating the team's performance.[[53]](#cite_note-IRB_BGRU_7-55) They are usually the first to receive the ball from the scrum-half following a breakdown, lineout, or scrum, and need to be decisive with what actions to take and be effective at communicating with the outside backs.[[53]](#cite_note-IRB_BGRU_7-55) Many fly-halves are also their team's goal kickers. The scrum-half is the link between the forwards and the backs.[[53]](#cite_note-IRB_BGRU_7-55) They receive the ball from the lineout and remove the ball from the back of the scrum, usually passing it to the fly-half.[[54]](#cite_note-IRB_BGRU_8-56) They also feed the scrum and sometimes have to act as a fourth loose forward.[[55]](#cite_note-57)

#### Three-quarters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_union&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Three-quarters")]

There are four three quarter positions: two [centres](/wiki/Rugby_union_positions#Centre "Rugby union positions") (inside and outside) and two [wings](/wiki/Rugby_union_positions#Wing "Rugby union positions") (left and right). In the Southern Hemisphere, the inside centre is commonly referred to as the second five-eighth, while the outside centre is simply known as the centre. The centres will attempt to tackle attacking players; whilst in attack, they should employ speed and strength to breach opposition defences.[[53]](#cite_note-IRB_BGRU_7-55) The wings are generally positioned on the outside of the backline. Their primary function is to finish off moves and score tries.[[56]](#cite_note-58) Wings are usually the fastest players in the team and are elusive runners who use their speed to avoid tackles.[[57]](#cite_note-59)

#### Full-back

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_union&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Full-back")]

The [full-back](/wiki/Rugby_union_positions#Full-back "Rugby union positions") is normally positioned several metres behind the back line. They often field opposition kicks and are usually the last line of defence should an opponent break through the back line.[[53]](#cite_note-IRB_BGRU_7-55) Two of the most important attributes of a good full-back are dependable catching skills and a good kicking game.[[58]](#cite_note-MacDonald-60)

## Laws and gameplay

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_union&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Laws and gameplay")]

Main articles: [Rugby union laws](/wiki/Rugby_union_laws "Rugby union laws") and [Rugby union gameplay](/wiki/Rugby_union_gameplay "Rugby union gameplay")

### Scoring

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_union&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Scoring")]

See also: [History of rugby union § Scoring](/wiki/History_of_rugby_union#Scoring "History of rugby union")

Rugby union is played between two teams – the one that scores more points wins the game. Points can be scored in several ways: a [try](/wiki/Try_(rugby) "Try (rugby)"), scored by grounding the ball in the in-goal area (between the goal line and the dead-ball line), is worth 5 points and a subsequent conversion kick scores 2 points; a successful [penalty kick](/wiki/Penalty_(rugby) "Penalty (rugby)") or a [drop goal](/wiki/Drop_goal "Drop goal") each score 3 points.[[59]](#cite_note-61) The values of each of these scoring methods have been changed over the years.[[60]](#cite_note-62)

### Playing field

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_union&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Playing field")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/RugbyPitchMetricDetailed.svg/250px-RugbyPitchMetricDetailed.svg.png)](/wiki/File:RugbyPitchMetricDetailed.svg)

Diagram of a rugby union playing field showing the various marked lines and distances

According to World Rugby's Laws of the Game,[[61]](#cite_note-63) a typical rugby ground, formally known as the "playing enclosure", is formed by two major zones:

* The "playing area", which includes the "field of play" and the two "in-goals", and
* The "perimeter area", a clear space, free of obstructions such as fences and other objects which could pose a danger to players and officials (but not including marker flags, which are typically of soft construction).

The referee (and their assistants) generally have full authority and responsibility for all players and other officials inside the playing enclosure. Fences or ropes (particularly at amateur clubs) are generally used to mark the extent of this area, although in modern stadia this may include the entire arena floor or other designated space.

The Laws, above all, require that the playing enclosure's surface be safe, whilst also permitting grass, sand, clay, snow or conforming artificial turf to be used; the surface would generally be uniform across both the playing area and perimeter area, although depending on how large the perimeter is, other surfaces such as dirt, artificial turf, etc. may be used outside of a "sliding" perimeter from the bounds of the playing area.

#### Playing area

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_union&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Playing area")]

For the most part, the "playing area" is where the majority of play occurs. The ball is generally considered live whilst in this area, so long as players do not infringe, with special rules applied to specific zones of the playing area.

The playing area consists of:

* The 'field of play", bounded by (but not including) the sidelines and goal-lines, and
* One "in-goal" area at each end of the field, each bounded by, but not including the extensions two parallel sidelines (known in this context as the "touch in-goal" lines) and the dead-ball line, and its other bound being the goal line (or "try line") which is included as part of the "in-goal" area.

##### Field of play

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_union&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Field of play")]

A typical "field of play" is generally 100 metres long by 68–70 metres wide for senior rugby, depending on the specific requirements of each ground. The Laws require the field of play to be between 94 and 100 metres (103 and 109 yards) long, with a width of between 68 and 70 metres (74 and 77 yards).

As other football codes, such as association football and rugby league, have specified a preferred or standard 68 metre width, this is often used unless a ground has been specifically designed to accommodate a 70-metre rugby field. 100 metres is the typical length, with a line (see below) often marked at halfway with "50" on it, representing 50 metres from each goal line. The variations have been allowed in the Laws, possibly to accommodate older grounds (perhaps even pre-metrification when yards and feet were specified) and developing nations.

##### Other lines and markings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_union&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Other lines and markings")]

The field of play is divided by a solid "halfway" line, drawn perpendicular to the sidelines at their midpoint. A 0.5m line is marked perpendicular to the halfway lines at its midpoint, designating the spot where the kickoffs shall be taken. The areas between each goal line and the halfway line are known as "halves" as in other football codes.

A pair of solid lines are also drawn perpendicular to the sidelines, 22 metres (formerly 25 yards) from each end of the field of play and called the 22-metre lines, or "22"s. An area at each end, also known as the "22", is bounded by, but does not include, the sidelines, goal line and 22-metre line. In this area, a defensive player who cleanly catches a ball kicked by the other team, without the ball having already touched the ground after the kick, is entitled to claim a free kick, or "[mark](/wiki/Mark_(rugby) "Mark (rugby)")". Additionally, if the ball is kicked into touch by a player who had at least one foot on or behind their own 22-metre line, the line-out formed at the spot where the ball crossed the touch-line instead of being taken in line with the spot from where it was kicked.[[62]](#cite_note-Touch19.1-64)

Additional broken or dashed lines (of 5 metre dash lengths, according to the Laws[[63]](#cite_note-65)) are drawn in each half or on each side of, the field, each with specific purposes under the Laws:

* "10-metre" lines: Dashed lines 10 metres either side of, and parallel to, the halfway line, designating the minimum distance a receiving team must retreat when receiving a kick-off, and the minimum distance a kick-off must travel to be legal. Equivalent to the 40-metre lines in rugby league but generally marked differently.
* "5-metre" lines: Dashed lines 5 metres into the field of play, parallel to each goal line. Scrums can be packed no nearer to each goal line than this line, and referees will often penalise scrum and ruck infringements in this area more harshly as defending sides will often try to stifle the attacking side's breakdown play.
* "Tram tracks/tramlines": Unnamed in the Laws and sometimes also referred to, confusingly, as the "5-metre" and "15-metre" lines, these two pairs of dashed lines are drawn parallel to each sideline, 5 metres and 15 metres, respectively, into the field of play from the nearer sideline, terminating at each of their respective ends' 5-metre line (parallel and adjacent to the goal line). The area between these lines are where players must stand when contesting a lineout throw.
* Additionally, the area between the two perpendicular sets of "5-metre" lines (i.e. 5 metres from each sideline and 5 metres from each goal line) is designated the "scrum zone". Where an offence occurs outside this area and the non-infringing side wishes to pack a scrum, the mark of the scrum will be moved into the zone by the referee.

Generally, points where the dashed lines intersect other lines will be marked with a "T" or cross shape, although the extensions of dashed lines are generally not drawn within 5 metres of the goal lines or sidelines, to allow a clear demarcation of the field of play's boundaries.

The Laws require the playing area to be rectangular in shape, however variations may be permitted with the approval of relevant unions. A notable example is Chatswood Oval in Sydney, Australia, an elliptically shaped cricket ground which is the home of Gordon rugby club, that has curved dead-ball lines to maximise the available in-goal space.

Where multiple sports share a field (e.g. a rugby league and a rugby union club sharing one field), lines may be overlaid on top of each other, sometimes in different colours. However, particularly for television, rugby union line markings are generally painted white. Some exceptions include the Wallabies (Australia's national team) who often have yellow markings. Local clubs may use black, yellow, or other colours on grass, with other surfaces possibly requiring different marking techniques.

Unlike association football, where on-field advertising is strictly forbidden in the laws,[[64]](#cite_note-66) World Rugby allows sponsors logos to be painted on the playing surface. This is another way in which clubs can make money in the professional era and is also often used by host nations, professional leagues and tournaments as additional revenue streams, particularly when games are broadcast. In recent years, augmented reality technology has been used to replace painting to protect the surface or save costs on painting fields, producing a similar effect for broadcast albeit sometimes with poorer results.[[65]](#cite_note-67)

#### In-goal areas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_union&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: In-goal areas")]

The in-goal areas sit behind the goal lines, equivalent to American football's "end zones". The in-goal areas must be between 6 and 22 metres (6.6 and 24.1 yards) deep and cover the full width of the field. A ball grounded in this area by an attacking player will generally result in a try being awarded, unless there has been a previous infringement, or the player has gone out-of-bounds whilst in possession of the ball.

#### Perimeter area

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_union&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Perimeter area")]

The perimeter area is considered "out-of-bounds" for the ball and the players, normally resulting in the non-infringing team receiving possession of the ball at a restart. The perimeter area can be divided into two areas:

* "Touch": The perimeter area beyond the sidelines of the playing area, but between the goal lines.
* "Touch-in-goal": The perimeter areas behind each goal line outside of the playing area. Some may refer to a ball which crosses the dead-ball lines as "dead", rather than touch-in-goal.

For the purposes of determining if a ball is "out-of-bounds" (i.e. has left the playing area), the perimeter area extends indefinitely away from the playing area.

When a ball or player goes into touch, a lineout throw is generally awarded to the opposition at the spot on the sideline where they left the field. Exceptions include a kick out "on the full" (i.e. the ball did not land in the field-of-play before going into touch) in which case the lineout would still take place on the sideline but back in line with where the ball was kicked, or when a team takes a free kick from a [penalty](/wiki/Penalty_card "Penalty card") where they would retain the right to throw-in.

The perimeter area should be clear and free of obstructions and heavy, solid objects which could pose a danger to players for at least 5 metres from the playing area, according to the Laws. Players often leave the playing area whether accidentally or due to being forced off of the field, sometimes sliding or needing to slow down from a sprint. Many venues at elite levels leave larger spaces around the field to accommodate fitter and faster (or heavier) players. Fixed cameras on tripods and advertising hoardings are often the main culprits for injuring players in the perimeter area.

##### Flag posts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_union&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Flag posts")]

Also required in the perimeter area are a set of 14 flag posts, each with a minimum height of 1.2 metres, marking the intersections of certain lines or other nominated distances. These are generally a plastic pole on a spring loaded or otherwise soft base, sometimes with a flag on top, covered in foam padding. Others may be moulded plastic or disposable cardboard. At lower levels, these flags may not be used, but are still specified in the Laws. Flags are placed as follows:

* One flag post at each intersection of the touch-in-goal lines and the goal-lines (four flags total)
* One flag post at each intersection of the touch-in-goal lines and the dead-ball lines (four flags total)
* One flag post positioned 2 metres outside of both of the sidelines, in line with both of the 22-metre lines (four flags total)
* One flag post positioned 2 metres outside of both of the sidelines, in line with the halfway line (two flags total)

#### Goalposts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_union&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Goalposts")]

Rugby goalposts are H-shaped and are situated in the middle of the goal lines at each end of the field. They consist of two vertical poles (known as "uprights"), generally made of steel or other metal but sometimes wood or a plastic, 5.6 metres (6.1 yd) apart, connected by a horizontal "crossbar" 3 metres (3.3 yd) above the ground. The minimum height for posts' uprights is 3.4 metres (3.7 yd),[[66]](#cite_note-pitch_and_post_dimensions-68) with taller posts generally seen. The bottom parts of each upright are generally wrapped in purpose-made padding to protect players from injury when coming into contact with the posts and creating another opportunity for sponsors. Until a Law change by World Rugby in 2020, it was possible for an attacking player to score a try by grounding the ball against the base of the upright as the padding was considered an extension of the goal line.[[67]](#cite_note-69)

### Match structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_union&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Match structure")]

At the beginning of the game, the captains and the referee toss a coin to decide which team will kick off first. Play then starts with a dropkick, with the players chasing the ball into the opposition's territory, and the other side trying to retrieve the ball and advance it. The dropkick must make contact with the ground before being kicked. If the ball does not reach the opponent's 10-metre (11-yard) line 10 meters away, the opposing team has two choices: to have the ball kicked off again, or to have a [scrum](/wiki/Scrum_(rugby_union) "Scrum (rugby union)") at the centre of the half-way line.[[68]](#cite_note-70)
If the player with the ball is tackled, frequently a [ruck](/wiki/Playing_rugby_union#Ruck "Playing rugby union") will result.[[69]](#cite_note-Midgley-71)

Games are divided into 40-minute halves, with an [intermission](/wiki/Half-time "Half-time") of not more than 15 minutes in the middle.[[70]](#cite_note-IRBLaw5-72) The sides exchange ends of the field after the half-time break.[[70]](#cite_note-IRBLaw5-72) Stoppages for injury or to allow the referee to take disciplinary action do not count as part of the playing time, so that the elapsed time is usually longer than 80 minutes.[[70]](#cite_note-IRBLaw5-72) The referee is responsible for keeping time, even when—as in many professional tournaments—he is assisted by an official time-keeper.[[70]](#cite_note-IRBLaw5-72) If time expires while the ball is in play, the game continues until the ball is "dead", and only then will the referee blow the whistle to signal half-time or full-time; but if the referee awards a penalty or free-kick, the game continues.[[70]](#cite_note-IRBLaw5-72)

In the knockout stages of rugby competitions, most notably the [Rugby World Cup](/wiki/Rugby_World_Cup "Rugby World Cup"), two extra time periods of 10 minutes periods are played (with an interval of 5 minutes in between) if the game is tied after full-time. If scores are level after 100 minutes then the rules call for 20 minutes of sudden-death extra time to be played. If the sudden-death extra time period results in no scoring a kicking competition is used to determine the winner. However, no match in the history of the Rugby World Cup has ever gone past 100 minutes into a sudden-death extra time period.[[71]](#cite_note-73)

### Passing and kicking

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_union&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Passing and kicking")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/2009_Isaac_Boss_cropped.jpg/250px-2009_Isaac_Boss_cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:2009_Isaac_Boss_cropped.jpg)

A player about to  
pass the ball

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Torneo_de_clasificaci%C3%B3n_WRWC_2014_-_Italia_vs_Espa%C3%B1a_-_86.jpg/250px-Torneo_de_clasificaci%C3%B3n_WRWC_2014_-_Italia_vs_Espa%C3%B1a_-_86.jpg)](/wiki/File:Torneo_de_clasificaci%C3%B3n_WRWC_2014_-_Italia_vs_Espa%C3%B1a_-_86.jpg)

Kicking conversion after a try

Forward passing (throwing the ball ahead to another player) is not allowed; the ball can be passed laterally or backwards.[[72]](#cite_note-irblaws12-74) The ball tends to be moved forward in three ways—by kicking, by a player running with it or within a scrum or [maul](/wiki/Playing_rugby_union#Maul "Playing rugby union"). Only the player with the ball may be tackled or rucked. A "knock-on" is committed when a player knocks the ball forward, and play is restarted with a scrum.[[72]](#cite_note-irblaws12-74)

Any player may kick the ball forward in an attempt to gain territory. When a player anywhere in the playing area kicks indirectly into [touch](/wiki/Touch_(rugby) "Touch (rugby)") so that the ball first bounces in the field of play, the throw-in is taken where the ball went into touch.[[73]](#cite_note-WRlaw19-75) If the player kicks directly into touch (i.e. without bouncing in-field first) from within one's own 22-metre (24-yard) line, the lineout is taken by the opposition where the ball went into touch, but if the ball is kicked into touch directly by a player outside the 22-metre (24-yard) line, the lineout is taken level to where the kick was taken.[[73]](#cite_note-WRlaw19-75)

### Breakdowns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_union&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Breakdowns")]

[![A child running away from camera in green and black hooped rugby jersey is being tackled around the hips and legs by another child in opposition kit.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Schoolkids_doing_a_rugby_tackle.jpg/250px-Schoolkids_doing_a_rugby_tackle.jpg)](/wiki/File:Schoolkids_doing_a_rugby_tackle.jpg)

A rugby tackle must be below the neck with the aim of impeding or grounding the player with the ball.

The aim of the defending side is to stop the player with the ball, by tackling them, and either bringing them to ground (which is frequently followed by a ruck) or contesting for possession with the ball-carrier on their feet (a maul). A ruck or maul is called a breakdown, and each is governed by a specific law.

**Tackling**

A player may tackle an opposing player who has the ball by holding them while bringing them to ground. Tacklers cannot tackle above the shoulder (the neck and head are out of bounds),[[74]](#cite_note-10.4e-76) and the tackler has to attempt to wrap their arms around the player being tackled to complete the tackle. It is illegal to push, shoulder-charge, or to trip a player using feet or legs, but hands may be used (this being referred to as a tap-tackle or [ankle-tap](/wiki/Ankle_tap "Ankle tap")).[[75]](#cite_note-77)[[76]](#cite_note-78) Tacklers may not tackle an opponent who has jumped to catch a ball until the player has landed.[[74]](#cite_note-10.4e-76)

**Rucking and Mauling**

Mauls occur after a player with the ball has come into contact with an opponent but the handler remains on his feet; once any combination of at least three players have bound themselves a maul has been set.[[51]](#cite_note-Glossary-53) A ruck is similar to the maul, but in this case the ball has gone to ground with at least three attacking players binding themselves on the ground in an attempt to secure the ball.[[51]](#cite_note-Glossary-53)

### Set pieces

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_union&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Set pieces")]

[![Two rows of opposing players, green to the fore, white behind, each aiding a jumping player from their team by lifting him towards an off-picture ball travelling overhead](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Ireland_vs_Georgia%2C_Rugby_World_Cup_2007_line_up.jpg/250px-Ireland_vs_Georgia%2C_Rugby_World_Cup_2007_line_up.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ireland_vs_Georgia,_Rugby_World_Cup_2007_line_up.jpg)

[Ireland](/wiki/Ireland_national_rugby_union_team "Ireland national rugby union team") and [Georgia](/wiki/Georgia_national_rugby_union_team "Georgia national rugby union team") contesting a line-out in the [2007 Rugby World Cup](/wiki/2007_Rugby_World_Cup "2007 Rugby World Cup")

#### Lineout

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_union&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Lineout")]

Main article: [Line-out (rugby union)](/wiki/Line-out_(rugby_union) "Line-out (rugby union)")

When the ball leaves the side of the field, a [line-out](/wiki/Line-out_(rugby_union) "Line-out (rugby union)") is awarded against the team which last touched the ball.[[77]](#cite_note-irblaws19-79) Forward players from each team line up a metre apart, perpendicular to the touchline and between 5 and 15 m (5.5 and 16.4 yd) from the touchline.[[77]](#cite_note-irblaws19-79) The ball is thrown from the touchline down the centre of the lines of forwards by a player (usually the [hooker](/wiki/Hooker_(rugby_union) "Hooker (rugby union)")) from the team that did not play the ball into touch.[[77]](#cite_note-irblaws19-79) The exception to this is when the ball went out from a penalty, in which case the side who gained the penalty throws the ball in.[[77]](#cite_note-irblaws19-79)

Both sides compete for the ball and players may lift their teammates.[[78]](#cite_note-80) A jumping player cannot be tackled until they stand and only shoulder-to-shoulder contact is allowed; deliberate infringement of this law is dangerous play, and results in a penalty kick.[[79]](#cite_note-81)

#### Scrum

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_union&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Scrum")]

Main article: [Scrum (rugby union)](/wiki/Scrum_(rugby_union) "Scrum (rugby union)")

[![Two opposing formations of eight men, in white and black to the left, red and black to the right, push against each other in a crouched position; behind them stands another player and the referee](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/Scrum-1.JPG/250px-Scrum-1.JPG)](/wiki/File:Scrum-1.JPG)

A [scrum](/wiki/Scrum_(rugby) "Scrum (rugby)") between New Zealand's [Crusaders](/wiki/Crusaders_(rugby_union) "Crusaders (rugby union)") and Australia's [Brumbies](/wiki/Brumbies "Brumbies")

A scrum is a way of restarting the game safely and fairly after a minor infringement.[[80]](#cite_note-WRlaw20-82) It is awarded when the ball has been knocked or passed forward, if a player takes the ball over their own try line and puts the ball down, when a player is accidentally [offside](/wiki/Offside_(rugby)#Rugby_union "Offside (rugby)") or when the ball is trapped in a ruck or maul with no realistic chance of being retrieved. A team may also opt for a scrum if awarded a penalty.[[80]](#cite_note-WRlaw20-82)

A scrum is formed by the eight forwards from each team crouching down and binding together in three rows, before interlocking with the opposing team.[[80]](#cite_note-WRlaw20-82) For each team, the front row consists of two props (loosehead and tighthead) either side of the hooker.[[80]](#cite_note-WRlaw20-82) The two props are typically amongst the strongest players on the team. The second row consists of two locks and the two flankers. Behind the second row is the number 8. This formation is known as the 3–4–1 formation.[[81]](#cite_note-FormingScrum-83) Once a scrum is formed the scrum-half from the team awarded the *feed* rolls the ball into the gap between the two front-rows known as the *tunnel*.[[80]](#cite_note-WRlaw20-82) The two hookers then compete for possession by hooking the ball backwards with their feet, while each pack tries to push the opposing pack backwards to help gain possession.[[80]](#cite_note-WRlaw20-82) The side that wins possession can either keep the ball under their feet while driving the opposition back, in order to gain ground, or transfer the ball to the back of the scrum where it can be picked up by the number 8 or by the scrum-half.[[80]](#cite_note-WRlaw20-82)

### Officials and offences

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_union&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Officials and offences")]

Main articles: [Rugby union match officials](/wiki/Rugby_union_match_officials "Rugby union match officials") and [Laws of rugby union](/wiki/Laws_of_rugby_union "Laws of rugby union")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/Touch_judge.jpg/250px-Touch_judge.jpg)](/wiki/File:Touch_judge.jpg)

Touch judge with flag

There are three match officials: a referee, and two assistant referees. The referees are commonly addressed as "Sir".[[82]](#cite_note-WRLaw6-84) The latter, formerly known as touch judges, had the primary function of indicating when the ball had gone into "touch"; their role has been expanded and they are now expected to assist the referee in a number of areas, such as watching for foul play and checking offside lines.[[82]](#cite_note-WRLaw6-84) In addition, for matches in high level competitions, there is often a [television match official](/wiki/Rugby_union_match_officials#Television_match_official_(TMO) "Rugby union match officials") (TMO; popularly called the "video referee"), to assist with certain decisions, linked up to the referee by radio.[[83]](#cite_note-85) The referees have a system of hand signals to indicate their decision.[[84]](#cite_note-86)

Common offences include tackling above the shoulders, collapsing a [scrum](/wiki/Scrum_(rugby_union) "Scrum (rugby union)"), ruck or maul, not releasing the ball when on the ground, or being offside.[[85]](#cite_note-WRLaw10-87) The non-offending team has a number of options when awarded a penalty: a "tap" kick, when the ball is kicked a very short distance from hand, allowing the kicker to regather the ball and run with it; a punt, when the ball is kicked a long distance from hand, for field position; a place-kick, when the kicker will attempt to score a goal; or a scrum.[[85]](#cite_note-WRLaw10-87) Players may be sent off (signalled by a [red card](/wiki/Red_card_(sports) "Red card (sports)")) or temporarily suspended ("sin-binned") for ten minutes ([yellow card](/wiki/Yellow_card_(sport) "Yellow card (sport)")) for foul play or repeated infringements, and may not be replaced.[[85]](#cite_note-WRLaw10-87)

Occasionally, infringements are not caught by the referee during the match and these may be "cited" by the [citing commissioner](/wiki/Citing_commissioner "Citing commissioner") after the match and have punishments (usually suspension for a number of weeks) imposed on the infringing player.[[86]](#cite_note-88)

### Replacements and substitutions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_union&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Replacements and substitutions")]

During the match, players may be replaced (for injury) or substituted (for tactical reasons).[[48]](#cite_note-Law3-50) A player who has been replaced may not rejoin play unless he was temporarily replaced to have bleeding controlled; a player who has been substituted may return temporarily, to replace a player who has a blood injury or has suffered a concussion, or permanently, if he is replacing a front-row forward.[[48]](#cite_note-Law3-50) In international matches, eight replacements are allowed; in domestic or cross-border tournaments, at the discretion of the responsible national union(s), the number of replacements may be nominated to a maximum of eight, of whom three must be sufficiently trained and experienced to provide cover for the three front row positions.[[48]](#cite_note-Law3-50)[[87]](#cite_note-Uncontested-89)

Prior to 2016, all substitutions, no matter the cause, counted against the limit during a match. In 2016, [World Rugby](/wiki/World_Rugby "World Rugby") changed the law so that substitutions made to replace a player deemed unable to continue due to foul play by the opposition would no longer count against the match limit. This change was introduced in January of that year in the Southern Hemisphere and June in the Northern Hemisphere.[[88]](#cite_note-90)

## Equipment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_union&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Equipment")]

Main article: [Rugby union equipment](/wiki/Rugby_union_equipment "Rugby union equipment")

The most basic items of equipment for a game of rugby union are the ball itself, a [rugby shirt](/wiki/Rugby_shirt "Rugby shirt") (also known as a "jersey"), [rugby shorts](/wiki/Rugby_shorts "Rugby shorts"), socks, and [boots](/wiki/Football_boot#Different_styles_for_different_surfaces "Football boot"). The [rugby ball](/wiki/Rugby_ball "Rugby ball") is oval in shape (technically a [prolate spheroid](/wiki/Prolate_spheroid "Prolate spheroid")), and is made up of four panels.[[89]](#cite_note-WRLaw2-91) The ball was historically made of leather, but in the modern era most games use a ball made from a synthetic material. World Rugby lays out specific dimensions for the ball, 280–300 mm (11–12 in) in length, 740–770 mm (29–30 in) in circumference of length and 580–620 mm (23–24 in) in circumference of width.[[89]](#cite_note-WRLaw2-91) Rugby boots have soles with studs to allow grip on the turf of the pitch. The studs may be either metal or plastic but must not have any sharp edges or ridges.[[90]](#cite_note-WRLaw4-92)

Protective equipment is optional and strictly regulated. The most common items are [mouthguards](/wiki/Mouthguard "Mouthguard"), which are worn by almost all players, and are compulsory in some rugby-playing nations.[[91]](#cite_note-93) Other protective items that are permitted include [headgear](/wiki/Scrum_cap "Scrum cap"); thin (not more than 10 mm thick), non-rigid shoulder pads and [shin guards](/wiki/Shin_guard "Shin guard"), which are worn underneath socks.[[90]](#cite_note-WRLaw4-92) Bandages or tape can be worn to support or protect injuries; some players wear tape around the head to protect the ears in scrums and rucks. Female players may also wear chest pads.[[90]](#cite_note-WRLaw4-92) Although not worn for protection, some types of fingerless mitts are allowed to aid grip.[[90]](#cite_note-WRLaw4-92)

It is the responsibility of the match officials to check players' clothing and equipment before a game to ensure that it conforms to the laws of the game.[[90]](#cite_note-WRLaw4-92)

## Governing bodies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_union&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Governing bodies")]

See also: [World Rugby](/wiki/World_Rugby "World Rugby") and [Timeline of foundation of national rugby unions](/wiki/Timeline_of_foundation_of_national_rugby_unions "Timeline of foundation of national rugby unions")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/World_Rugby_member_unions_and_associates.svg/500px-World_Rugby_member_unions_and_associates.svg.png)](/wiki/File:World_Rugby_member_unions_and_associates.svg)

**Member and Associated Unions**

Member Union

Associated Union

The international governing body of rugby union (and associated games such as sevens) is World Rugby.[[92]](#cite_note-IRB_Organisation-94) The World Rugby headquarters are in [Dublin](/wiki/Dublin "Dublin"), Ireland.[[92]](#cite_note-IRB_Organisation-94) World Rugby, founded in 1886, governs the sport worldwide and publishes the game's laws and [rankings](/wiki/IRB_World_Rankings "IRB World Rankings").[[92]](#cite_note-IRB_Organisation-94) As of February 2014, World Rugby (then known as the IRB, for International Rugby Board) recorded 119 unions in its membership, 101 full members and 18 associate member countries.[[2]](#cite_note-6.6m-2) According to World Rugby, rugby union is played by men and women in over 100 countries.[[92]](#cite_note-IRB_Organisation-94) World Rugby controls the [Rugby World Cup](/wiki/Rugby_World_Cup "Rugby World Cup"),[[92]](#cite_note-IRB_Organisation-94) the [Women's Rugby World Cup](/wiki/Women%27s_Rugby_World_Cup "Women's Rugby World Cup"),[[93]](#cite_note-95) [Rugby World Cup Sevens](/wiki/Rugby_World_Cup_Sevens "Rugby World Cup Sevens"),[[94]](#cite_note-96) [HSBC Sevens Series](/wiki/World_Rugby_Sevens_Series "World Rugby Sevens Series"),[[95]](#cite_note-97) [HSBC Women's Sevens Series](/wiki/World_Rugby_Women%27s_Sevens_Series "World Rugby Women's Sevens Series"),[[96]](#cite_note-98) [World Under 20 Championship](/wiki/World_Rugby_Under_20_Championship "World Rugby Under 20 Championship"),[[97]](#cite_note-99) [World Under 20 Trophy](/wiki/World_Rugby_Under_20_Trophy "World Rugby Under 20 Trophy"),[[98]](#cite_note-100) [Nations Cup](/wiki/World_Rugby_Nations_Cup_(2006%E2%80%932019) "World Rugby Nations Cup (2006–2019)")[[99]](#cite_note-101) and the [Pacific Nations Cup](/wiki/Pacific_Nations_Cup "Pacific Nations Cup").[[100]](#cite_note-102) World Rugby holds votes to decide where each of these events are to be held, except in the case of the Sevens World Series for men and women, for which World Rugby contracts with several national unions to hold individual events.

Six regional associations, which are members of World Rugby, form the next level of administration; these are:

* [Rugby Africa](/wiki/Rugby_Africa "Rugby Africa"), formerly Confederation of African Rugby (CAR)[[101]](#cite_note-103)
* [Asia Rugby](/wiki/Asia_Rugby "Asia Rugby"), formerly Asian Rugby Football Union (ARFU)[[102]](#cite_note-104)
* [Rugby Americas North](/wiki/Rugby_Americas_North "Rugby Americas North"), formerly North America Caribbean Rugby Association (NACRA)[[103]](#cite_note-105)
* [Rugby Europe](/wiki/Rugby_Europe "Rugby Europe"), previously Fédération Internationale de Rugby Amateur – Association Européenne de Rugby (FIRA-AER)[[104]](#cite_note-FIRA-106)
* [Oceania Rugby](/wiki/Oceania_Rugby "Oceania Rugby"), formerly Federation of Oceania Rugby Unions (FORU)[[105]](#cite_note-107)
* [Sudamérica Rugby](/wiki/Sudam%C3%A9rica_Rugby "Sudamérica Rugby"), formerly Confederación Sudamericana de Rugby (*South American Rugby Confederation*, or CONSUR)[[106]](#cite_note-108)

[SANZAAR](/wiki/SANZAAR "SANZAAR") (South Africa, New Zealand, Australia and Argentina Rugby) is a joint venture of the [South African Rugby Union](/wiki/South_African_Rugby_Union "South African Rugby Union"), [New Zealand Rugby](/wiki/New_Zealand_Rugby "New Zealand Rugby"), [Rugby Australia](/wiki/Rugby_Australia "Rugby Australia") and the [Argentine Rugby Union](/wiki/Argentine_Rugby_Union "Argentine Rugby Union") (UAR) that operates [Super Rugby](/wiki/Super_Rugby "Super Rugby") and [The Rugby Championship](/wiki/The_Rugby_Championship "The Rugby Championship") (formerly the Tri Nations before the entry of Argentina).[[107]](#cite_note-109) Although UAR initially had no representation on the former SANZAR board, it was granted input into the organisation's issues, especially with regard to The Rugby Championship,[[108]](#cite_note-110) and became a full SANZAAR member in 2016 (when the country entered Super Rugby).

National unions oversee rugby union within individual countries and are affiliated to World Rugby. Since 2016, the World Rugby Council has 40 seats. A total of 11 unions—the eight foundation unions of [England](/wiki/Rugby_Football_Union "Rugby Football Union"), [Scotland](/wiki/Scottish_Rugby_Union "Scottish Rugby Union"), [Ireland](/wiki/Irish_Rugby_Union "Irish Rugby Union"), [Wales](/wiki/Welsh_Rugby_Union "Welsh Rugby Union"), Australia, New Zealand, South Africa and [France](/wiki/French_Rugby_Federation "French Rugby Federation"), plus Argentina, [Canada](/wiki/Rugby_Canada "Rugby Canada") and [Italy](/wiki/Italian_Rugby_Federation "Italian Rugby Federation")—have two seats each. In addition, the six regional associations have two seats each. Four more unions—[Georgia](/wiki/Georgia_Rugby_Union "Georgia Rugby Union"), [Japan](/wiki/Japan_Rugby_Football_Union "Japan Rugby Football Union"), [Romania](/wiki/Romanian_Rugby_Federation "Romanian Rugby Federation") and the [USA](/wiki/USA_Rugby "USA Rugby")—have one seat each. Finally, the chairman and vice chairman, who usually come from one of the eight foundation unions (although the current vice chairman, [Agustín Pichot](/wiki/Agust%C3%ADn_Pichot "Agustín Pichot"), is with the non-foundation Argentine union) have one vote each.[[109]](#cite_note-111)[[92]](#cite_note-IRB_Organisation-94)

## Global reach

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_union&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Global reach")]

See also: [List of rugby union playing countries](/wiki/List_of_rugby_union_playing_countries "List of rugby union playing countries")

[![A group of thirteen supporters pose together, nine standing in back row, four seated at front, some wearing rugby jerseys and others sporting traditional Japanese costumes and Japanese flags.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Walers_Japan_Rugby_World_Cup_2007_09_20_supporters1.jpg/250px-Walers_Japan_Rugby_World_Cup_2007_09_20_supporters1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Walers_Japan_Rugby_World_Cup_2007_09_20_supporters1.jpg)

Japanese and Welsh rugby fans in [Cardiff](/wiki/Cardiff "Cardiff"), Wales, September 2007

The earliest countries to adopt rugby union were [England](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_England "Rugby union in England"), the country of inception, and the other three Home Nations, [Scotland](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Scotland "Rugby union in Scotland"), [Ireland](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Ireland "Rugby union in Ireland") and [Wales](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Wales "Rugby union in Wales"). The spread of rugby union as a global sport has its roots in the exporting of the game by British expatriates, military personnel, and overseas university students. The first rugby club in [France](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_France "Rugby union in France") was formed by British residents in Le Havre in 1872, while the next year [Argentina](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Argentina "Rugby union in Argentina") recorded its first game: 'Banks' v 'City' in Buenos Aires.[[110]](#cite_note-Godwin11-112) In turn, French and Argentine travellers, particularly students, spread the Game beyond the British Empire, forming roots in particular in Italy, Romania, Uruguay and Chile.

Eight countries have adopted rugby union as their de facto [national sport](/wiki/National_sport "National sport"); they are [Fiji](/wiki/Fiji "Fiji"),[[111]](#cite_note-113) [Georgia](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Georgia "Rugby union in Georgia"), [Madagascar](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Madagascar "Rugby union in Madagascar"),[[112]](#cite_note-114)[[113]](#cite_note-115)[[114]](#cite_note-116) New Zealand,[[115]](#cite_note-117) [Samoa](/wiki/Samoa "Samoa"),[[116]](#cite_note-118) [Tonga](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Tonga "Rugby union in Tonga"),[[117]](#cite_note-119) Wales and South Africa.[[118]](#cite_note-120)

### Oceania

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_union&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Oceania")]

A rugby club was formed in Sydney, [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales"), [Australia](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Australia "Rugby union in Australia") in 1864; while the sport was said to have been introduced to [New Zealand](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_New_Zealand "Rugby union in New Zealand") by [Charles Monro](/wiki/Charles_John_Monro "Charles John Monro") in 1870, who played rugby while a student at [Christ's College, Finchley](/wiki/Christ%27s_College_(Finchley) "Christ's College (Finchley)").[[15]](#cite_note-Godwin10-16)

Several island nations have embraced the sport of rugby. Rugby was first played in [Fiji](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Fiji "Rugby union in Fiji") c. 1884 by European and Fijian soldiers of the Native Constabulary at [Ba](/wiki/Ba_(town) "Ba (town)") on Viti Levu island.[[119]](#cite_note-121)[[120]](#cite_note-122) Fiji then sent their first overseas team to [Samoa](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Samoa "Rugby union in Samoa") in 1924, who in turn set up their own [union](/wiki/Lakapi_Samoa "Lakapi Samoa") in 1924.[[121]](#cite_note-123) Along with [Tonga](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Tonga "Rugby union in Tonga"), other countries to have national rugby teams in Oceania include [the Cook Islands](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_the_Cook_Islands "Rugby union in the Cook Islands"), [Niue](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Niue "Rugby union in Niue"), [Papua New Guinea](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Papua_New_Guinea "Rugby union in Papua New Guinea") and [Solomon Islands](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Solomon_Islands "Rugby union in Solomon Islands").[[122]](#cite_note-124)

### North America and Caribbean

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_union&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: North America and Caribbean")]

See also: [Rugby Americas North](/wiki/Rugby_Americas_North "Rugby Americas North")

In North America, a club formed in Montreal in 1868, [Canada](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Canada "Rugby union in Canada")'s first club. The city of Montreal also played its part in the introduction of the sport in the [United States](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_the_United_States "Rugby union in the United States"), when students of [McGill University](/wiki/McGill_University "McGill University") played against a team from [Harvard University](/wiki/Harvard_University "Harvard University") in 1874.[[15]](#cite_note-Godwin10-16)[[110]](#cite_note-Godwin11-112) The two variants of gridiron football — [Canadian football](/wiki/Canadian_football "Canadian football") and, to a lesser extent, [American football](/wiki/American_football "American football") — were once considered forms of [rugby football](/wiki/Rugby_football "Rugby football"), but the introduction of the legal [forward pass](/wiki/Forward_pass "Forward pass") severed the conceptual link between the old world rugby codes and new world gridiron codes, and they are seldom now referred to as forms of rugby football. One lasting element of the link, the governing body of Canadian football, [Football Canada](/wiki/Football_Canada#1880–1955,_Canadian_Rugby_Union "Football Canada"), was known as the Canadian Rugby Union (CRU) as late as 1967, more than fifty years after the sport parted ways with the established rules of rugby union. The [Grey Cup](/wiki/Grey_Cup "Grey Cup"), the trophy awarded to the victorious team playing in the namesake championship of the professional [Canadian Football League](/wiki/Canadian_Football_League "Canadian Football League") (CFL), was originally awarded to the champion of the CRU. The two strongest leagues in the CRU, the [Interprovincial Rugby Football Union](/wiki/Interprovincial_Rugby_Football_Union "Interprovincial Rugby Football Union") in [Eastern Canada](/wiki/Eastern_Canada "Eastern Canada") and the [Western Interprovincial Football Union](/wiki/Western_Interprovincial_Football_Union "Western Interprovincial Football Union") in [Western Canada](/wiki/Western_Canada "Western Canada"), evolved into the present day CFL.

Although the exact date of arrival of [rugby union in Trinidad and Tobago](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Trinidad_and_Tobago "Rugby union in Trinidad and Tobago") is unknown, their first club Northern RFC was formed in 1923, a national team was playing by 1927 and due to a cancelled tour to [British Guiana](/wiki/British_Guiana "British Guiana") in 1933, switched their venue to [Barbados](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Barbados "Rugby union in Barbados"); introducing rugby to the island.[[123]](#cite_note-125)[[124]](#cite_note-126) Other Atlantic countries to play rugby union include [Jamaica](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Jamaica "Rugby union in Jamaica")[[125]](#cite_note-127) and [Bermuda](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Bermuda "Rugby union in Bermuda").[[126]](#cite_note-128)

[Rugby union](/wiki/College_rugby "College rugby") is the fastest growing [college sport](/wiki/College_sport "College sport") and sport in general in the US.[[5]](#cite_note-urlU.S_Rugby_Scholarships_-_U.S._Sports_Scholarships-6)[[6]](#cite_note-urlRugby:_Fastest_growing_sport_in_the_U.S._also_one_of_the_oldest_-_Global_Sport_Matters-7)[[7]](#cite_note-urlWhere_Is_Rugby_the_Most_Popular_Among_Students:_Comparison_of_US_and_UK_Student_Leagues_{{!}}_Love_Rugby_League-8)

[Major League Rugby](/wiki/Major_League_Rugby "Major League Rugby") is the top professional rugby union competition in the US and Canada. One American professional team, [American Raptors](/wiki/American_Raptors "American Raptors"), formerly the MLR Glendale and Colorado Raptors, plays in the broadly South American [Super Rugby Americas](/wiki/Super_Rugby_Americas "Super Rugby Americas") competition.

### Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_union&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Europe")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Germany_vs_Belgium_rugby_match.jpg/250px-Germany_vs_Belgium_rugby_match.jpg)](/wiki/File:Germany_vs_Belgium_rugby_match.jpg)

[Germany](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Germany "Rugby union in Germany") playing [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium_national_rugby_union_team "Belgium national rugby union team") in a World Cup qualifier, April 2006

The growth of rugby union in [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe") outside the 6 Nations countries in terms of playing numbers, [attendances](/wiki/Attendance "Attendance"), and viewership has been sporadic. Historically, British and Irish home teams played the Southern Hemisphere teams of Australia, New Zealand, and South Africa, as well as France. The rest of Europe were left to play amongst themselves. During a period when it had been isolated by the British and Irish Unions, France, lacking international competition, became the only European team from the top tier to regularly play the other European countries; mainly [Belgium](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Belgium "Rugby union in Belgium"), the [Netherlands](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_the_Netherlands "Rugby union in the Netherlands"), [Germany](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Germany "Rugby union in Germany"), [Spain](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Spain "Rugby union in Spain"), [Romania](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Romania "Rugby union in Romania"), [Poland](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Poland "Rugby union in Poland"), [Italy](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Italy "Rugby union in Italy") and [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Czechoslovakia "Rugby union in Czechoslovakia").[[104]](#cite_note-FIRA-106)[[127]](#cite_note-129) In 1934, instigated by the [French Rugby Federation](/wiki/French_Rugby_Federation "French Rugby Federation"), FIRA (Fédération Internationale de Rugby Amateur) was formed to organise rugby union outside the authority of the IRFB.[[104]](#cite_note-FIRA-106) The founding members were [Italy](/wiki/Italian_Rugby_Federation "Italian Rugby Federation"), [Romania](/wiki/Romanian_Rugby_Federation "Romanian Rugby Federation"), [Netherlands](/wiki/Dutch_Rugby_Union "Dutch Rugby Union"), [Portugal](/wiki/Portuguese_Rugby_Federation "Portuguese Rugby Federation"), [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovak_Rugby_Union "Czechoslovak Rugby Union"), and [Sweden](/wiki/Swedish_Rugby_Union "Swedish Rugby Union").

Four of these teams have had, at different times, notable success; the most successful, [Italy](/wiki/Italy_national_rugby_union_team "Italy national rugby union team"), joined the [Five Nations Championship](/wiki/Five_Nations_Championship "Five Nations Championship") in 2000 as its sixth member, it has recorded numerous wins over all its 6N rivals with the exception of England, as well as defeating South Africa, Australia and Argentina in official matches. Before Italy's ascension, Romania enjoyed a period of success as their teams, backed by a supportive Communist government, regularly challenged the major European nations. The fall of Communism in Romania led to an era of steep decline from which Romania has yet to fully emerge, but it remains one of the most prominent of the second level European teams. Georgia, in the Caucasus, rose to prominence in the early 21st century; the traditional national game [lelo](/wiki/Lelo_burti "Lelo burti") bore strong similarities to rugby union, meaning the country was naturally attracted to the sport upon independence, and the Georgians are now regarded as the top European nation below the traditional Six Nations, and even possibly on a par with Italy. Having qualified for the [2023 Rugby World Cup](/wiki/2023_Rugby_World_Cup "2023 Rugby World Cup") through the final repechage, [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal_national_rugby_union_team "Portugal national rugby union team") had a startlingly successful tournament, holding Georgia to an 18–18 draw, and performing strongly against Tier One sides Wales and Australia before an historic victory over eventual quarter-finalists [Fiji](/wiki/Fiji_national_rugby_union_team "Fiji national rugby union team"). Portugal demonstrated significant fan support during the tournament.

Other European rugby playing nations of note include [Russia](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Russia "Rugby union in Russia"), whose first officially recorded match is marked by an encounter between [Dynamo Moscow](/wiki/Dynamo_Moscow "Dynamo Moscow") and the [Moscow Institute of Physical Education](/wiki/Moscow_Institute_of_Physical_Education "Moscow Institute of Physical Education") in 1933.[[128]](#cite_note-130) [Rugby union in Spain](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Spain "Rugby union in Spain"), often associated with the Basque and Catalan regions near the French border, is also relatively strong in this context, having twice missed out on World Cup qualification by rule breaches having gained the points required, and hosting several large crowds for [European Professional Rugby Cup](/wiki/European_Rugby_Champions_Cup "European Rugby Champions Cup") matches.

In 1999, FIRA agreed to place itself under the auspices of the IRB, transforming itself into a strictly European organising body.
