Accordingly, it changed its name to FIRA–AER (Fédération Internationale de Rugby Amateur – Association Européenne de Rugby). It adopted its current name of [Rugby Europe](/wiki/Rugby_Europe "Rugby Europe") in 2014.

### South America

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_union&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: South America")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/URBA_Finals.jpg/250px-URBA_Finals.jpg)](/wiki/File:URBA_Finals.jpg)

Argentine teams [Alumni](/wiki/Asociaci%C3%B3n_Alumni "Asociación Alumni") and [Hindú](/wiki/Hind%C3%BA_Club "Hindú Club") playing the [Torneo de la URBA](/wiki/Torneo_de_la_URBA "Torneo de la URBA") final match, 2007

Although [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina") is the best-known rugby playing nation in [South America](/wiki/South_America "South America"), founding the [Argentine Rugby Union](/wiki/Argentine_Rugby_Union "Argentine Rugby Union") in 1899,[[129]](#cite_note-131) several other countries on the continent have a long history. Rugby had been played in [Brazil](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Brazil "Rugby union in Brazil") since the end of the 19th century, but the game was played regularly only from 1926, when São Paulo beat Santos in an inter-city match.[[130]](#cite_note-132) It took [Uruguay](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Uruguay "Rugby union in Uruguay") several aborted attempts to adapt to rugby, led mainly by the efforts of the [Montevideo Cricket Club](/wiki/Montevideo_Cricket_Club "Montevideo Cricket Club"); these efforts succeeded in 1951 with the formation of a national league and four clubs.[[131]](#cite_note-133) Other South American countries that formed a rugby union include [Chile](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Chile "Rugby union in Chile") (1948),[[132]](#cite_note-134) and [Paraguay](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Paraguay "Rugby union in Paraguay") (1968).[[133]](#cite_note-135)

[Super Rugby Americas](/wiki/S%C3%BAper_Liga_Americana_de_Rugby "Súper Liga Americana de Rugby") is the professional Rugby union competition in [South America](/wiki/South_America "South America").

### Asia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_union&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Asia")]

Main article: [Rugby union in Asia](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Asia "Rugby union in Asia")

Many Asian countries have a tradition of playing rugby dating from the British Empire. [India](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_India "Rugby union in India") began playing rugby in the early 1870s, the [Calcutta Football Club](/wiki/Calcutta_Football_Club "Calcutta Football Club") forming in 1873. However, with the departure of a local British army regiment, interest in rugby diminished in the area.[[134]](#cite_note-136) In 1878, The Calcutta Football Club was disbanded, and rugby in India faltered.[[135]](#cite_note-137) [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Sri_Lanka "Rugby union in Sri Lanka") claims to have founded their union in 1878, and although little official information from the period is available, the team won the All-India cup in Madras in 1920.[[136]](#cite_note-138) The first recorded match in [Malaysia](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Malaysia "Rugby union in Malaysia") was in 1892, but the first confirmation of rugby is the existence of the HMS Malaya Cup which was first presented in 1922 and is still awarded to the winners of the Malay sevens.[[137]](#cite_note-139)

Rugby union was introduced to [Japan](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Japan "Rugby union in Japan") in 1899 by two Cambridge students: [Ginnosuke Tanaka](/wiki/Ginnosuke_Tanaka "Ginnosuke Tanaka") and [Edward Bramwell Clarke](/wiki/Edward_Bramwell_Clarke "Edward Bramwell Clarke").[[138]](#cite_note-140)[[139]](#cite_note-141) The Japan RFU was founded in 1926 and its place in rugby history was cemented when Japan hosted the [2019 World Cup](/wiki/2019_Rugby_World_Cup "2019 Rugby World Cup").[[140]](#cite_note-2015_bid-142) It was the first country outside the Commonwealth, Ireland and France to host the event, and was viewed by the IRB as an opportunity for rugby union to extend its reach,[[140]](#cite_note-2015_bid-142) particularly in Asia. Other Asian playing countries of note include [Singapore](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Singapore "Rugby union in Singapore"), [South Korea](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_South_Korea "Rugby union in South Korea"), [China](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_China "Rugby union in China") and [The Philippines](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_the_Philippines "Rugby union in the Philippines"), while the former British colony of [Hong Kong](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Hong_Kong "Rugby union in Hong Kong") is notable within rugby for its development of the rugby sevens game, especially the [Hong Kong Sevens](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Sevens "Hong Kong Sevens") tournament which was founded in 1976.[[141]](#cite_note-143)

Rugby in the Middle East and the Gulf States began in the 1950s, with clubs formed by British and French Services stationed in the region after the Second World War.[[142]](#cite_note-144) When these servicemen left, the clubs and teams were kept alive by young professionals, mostly Europeans, working in these countries. The official union of [Oman](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Oman "Rugby union in Oman") was formed in 1971.[[143]](#cite_note-145) [Bahrain](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Bahrain "Rugby union in Bahrain") founded its union a year later, while in 1975 the [Dubai Sevens](/wiki/Dubai_Sevens "Dubai Sevens"), the Gulf's leading rugby tournament, was created. Rugby remains a minority sport in the region with [Israel](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Israel "Rugby union in Israel") and the [United Arab Emirates](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_union_in_United_Arab_Emirates&action=edit&redlink=1 "Rugby union in United Arab Emirates (page does not exist)"), as of 2019, being the only member unions from the Middle East to be included in the [IRB World Rankings](/wiki/IRB_World_Rankings "IRB World Rankings").[[144]](#cite_note-Full_rankings-146)

[![A close-up shot of the Ivory Coast players, in their country's orange jerseys, entering the field from the dressing room tunnel](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/Ivory_Coast_rugby_union_team_in_2008.jpg/250px-Ivory_Coast_rugby_union_team_in_2008.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ivory_Coast_rugby_union_team_in_2008.jpg)

[Ivory Coast](/wiki/Ivory_Coast "Ivory Coast") before their [2011 World Cup qualifier](/wiki/2011_Rugby_World_Cup_%E2%80%93_Africa_qualification "2011 Rugby World Cup – Africa qualification") vs. Zambia, 21 July 2008

### Africa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_union&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Africa")]

In 1875, rugby was introduced to [South Africa](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_South_Africa "Rugby union in South Africa") by British soldiers garrisoned in Cape Town.[[110]](#cite_note-Godwin11-112) The game spread quickly across the country, displacing [Winchester College football](/wiki/Winchester_College_football "Winchester College football") as the sport of choice in South Africa and spreading to nearby [Zimbabwe](/wiki/Zimbabwe "Zimbabwe"). South African settlers also brought the game with them to Namibia and competed against British administrators in [British East Africa](/wiki/British_East_Africa "British East Africa"). During the late 19th and early 20th century, the sport in Africa was spread by settlers and colonials who often adopted a "whites-only" policy to playing the game. This resulted in rugby being viewed as a [bourgeois](/wiki/Bourgeois "Bourgeois") sport by the indigenous people with limited appeal.[[145]](#cite_note-147) Despite this, enclaves of black participation developed notably in the [Eastern Cape](/wiki/Eastern_Cape "Eastern Cape") and in [Harare](/wiki/Harare "Harare"). The earliest countries to see the playing of competitive rugby include South Africa, and neighbouring [Rhodesia](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Rhodesia "Rugby union in Rhodesia") (modern-day Zimbabwe), which formed the Rhodesia Rugby Football Union in 1895 and became a regular stop for touring British and New Zealand sides.[[146]](#cite_note-148)

In more recent times, the sport has been embraced by several African nations. In the early 21st century Madagascar has experienced crowds of 40,000 at national matches,[[147]](#cite_note-149) while Namibia, whose history of rugby can be dated from 1915, have qualified for the final stages of the World Cup seven times since 1999.[[148]](#cite_note-150) Other African nations to be represented in the World Rugby Rankings as Member Unions include [Côte d'Ivoire](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_C%C3%B4te_d%27Ivoire "Rugby union in Côte d'Ivoire"), [Kenya](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Kenya "Rugby union in Kenya"), [Uganda](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Uganda "Rugby union in Uganda") and [Zambia](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Zambia "Rugby union in Zambia").[[144]](#cite_note-Full_rankings-146) South Africa and Kenya are among the 15 "core teams" that participate in every event of the men's [World Rugby Sevens Series](/wiki/World_Rugby_Sevens_Series "World Rugby Sevens Series").[[149]](#cite_note-151)

## Women's rugby union

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_union&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Women's rugby union")]

Main article: [Women's rugby union](/wiki/Women%27s_rugby_union "Women's rugby union")

[![A female player in yellow and green kit and wearing a white scrum cap, jumps to collect a ball while supported by teammates.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Women%27s_Rugby_Mark.jpg/250px-Women%27s_Rugby_Mark.jpg)](/wiki/File:Women%27s_Rugby_Mark.jpg)

US women's rugby:  
NC Hustlers vs. Midwest II

Records of women's rugby football date from the late 19th century, with the first documented source being [Emily Valentine](/wiki/Emily_Valentine "Emily Valentine")'s writings, in which she states that she set up a rugby team in [Portora Royal School](/wiki/Portora_Royal_School "Portora Royal School") in Enniskillen, Ireland in 1887.[[150]](#cite_note-152) Although there are reports of early women's matches in New Zealand and France, one of the first notable games to prove primary evidence was the 1917 war-time encounter between Cardiff Ladies and Newport Ladies; a photo of which shows the Cardiff team before the match at the [Cardiff Arms Park](/wiki/Cardiff_Arms_Park "Cardiff Arms Park").[[151]](#cite_note-153) Since the 1980s, the game has grown in popularity among female athletes, and by 2010, according to [World Rugby](/wiki/World_Rugby "World Rugby"), women's rugby was being played in over 100 countries.[[152]](#cite_note-154)

The English-based Women's Rugby Football Union (WRFU), responsible for women's rugby in England, Scotland, Ireland, and Wales, was founded in 1983, and is the oldest formally organised national governing body for women's rugby. This was replaced in 1994 by the [Rugby Football Union for Women](/wiki/Rugby_Football_Union_for_Women "Rugby Football Union for Women") (RFUW) in England with each of the other Home Nations governing their own countries.[[153]](#cite_note-Women's_Rugby-155)

The premier international competition in rugby union for women is the [Women's Rugby World Cup](/wiki/Women%27s_Rugby_World_Cup "Women's Rugby World Cup"), first held in 1991; from 1994 through 2014, it was held every four years.[[153]](#cite_note-Women's_Rugby-155) After the 2014 event, the tournament was brought forward a year to 2017 to avoid clashing with other sporting cycles, in particular the [Rugby World Cup Sevens](/wiki/Rugby_World_Cup_Sevens "Rugby World Cup Sevens") competition.[[154]](#cite_note-156) The Women's Rugby World Cup returned to a four-year cycle after 2017, with future competitions to be held in the middle year of the men's World Cup cycle.

## Major international competitions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_union&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Major international competitions")]

Further information: [List of rugby union competitions](/wiki/List_of_rugby_union_competitions "List of rugby union competitions")

### Rugby World Cup

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_union&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Rugby World Cup")]

Main article: [Rugby World Cup](/wiki/Rugby_World_Cup "Rugby World Cup")

[![An avenue of trees leads to a large iron lattice tower, in which an oversized rugby ball hangs within the lower sections.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Coupe_du_monde_rugby_-_tour_Eiffel.JPG/250px-Coupe_du_monde_rugby_-_tour_Eiffel.JPG)](/wiki/File:Coupe_du_monde_rugby_-_tour_Eiffel.JPG)

A giant rugby ball suspended from the [Eiffel Tower](/wiki/Eiffel_Tower "Eiffel Tower") to commemorate France's hosting of the [2007 World Cup](/wiki/2007_Rugby_World_Cup "2007 Rugby World Cup")

The most important competition in rugby union is the [Rugby World Cup](/wiki/Rugby_World_Cup "Rugby World Cup"), a men's tournament that has taken place every four years since the inaugural event in 1987. [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa_national_rugby_union_team "South Africa national rugby union team") are the reigning champions, having defeated New Zealand 11–12 in the final of the [2023 Rugby World Cup](/wiki/2023_Rugby_World_Cup "2023 Rugby World Cup") in France.[[155]](#cite_note-Rugby_World_Cup_France_2023-157) South Africa has the highest number of World Cup wins: 4 (1995, 2007, 2019 and 2023). [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand_national_rugby_union_team "New Zealand national rugby union team") won the title three times (1987, 2011 and 2015), Australia have won twice (1991 and 1999), and England once (2003). England is the only team from the Northern Hemisphere to have won the Rugby World Cup.[[156]](#cite_note-rugby_world_cup-158)

The Rugby World Cup has continued to grow since its inception in 1987. The first tournament, in which 16 teams competed for the title, was broadcast to 17 countries with an accumulated total of 230 million television viewers. Ticket sales during the pool stages and finals of the same tournament was less than a million. The [2007 World Cup](/wiki/2007_Rugby_World_Cup "2007 Rugby World Cup") was contested by 94 countries with ticket sales of 3,850,000 over the pool and final stage. The accumulated television audience for the event, then broadcast to 200 countries, was a claimed 4.2 billion.[[157]](#cite_note-IRB2010-159)

The 2019 Rugby World Cup took place in Japan between 20 September and 2 November. It was the ninth edition and the first time the tournament has been held in Asia.[[158]](#cite_note-Rugby_World_Cup_Japan_2019-160)

### Regional tournaments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_union&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Regional tournaments")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Griffins_RFC_Kotka_1.jpg/250px-Griffins_RFC_Kotka_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Griffins_RFC_Kotka_1.jpg)

Griffins RFC Kotka, the rugby union team from [Kotka](/wiki/Kotka "Kotka"), [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland"), playing in the Rugby-7 Tournament in 2013

Major international competitions are the [Six Nations Championship](/wiki/Six_Nations_Championship "Six Nations Championship") and [The Rugby Championship](/wiki/The_Rugby_Championship "The Rugby Championship"), held in Europe and the Southern Hemisphere respectively.[[159]](#cite_note-161)

The Six Nations is an annual competition involving the European teams [England](/wiki/England_national_rugby_union_team "England national rugby union team"), [France](/wiki/France_national_rugby_union_team "France national rugby union team"), [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland_national_rugby_union_team "Ireland national rugby union team"), [Italy](/wiki/Italy_national_rugby_union_team "Italy national rugby union team"), [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland_national_rugby_union_team "Scotland national rugby union team") and [Wales](/wiki/Wales_national_rugby_union_team "Wales national rugby union team").[[160]](#cite_note-SixNations-162) Each country plays the other five once. Following the first internationals between England and Scotland, Ireland and Wales began competing in the 1880s, forming the *Home International Championships*.[[160]](#cite_note-SixNations-162) France joined the tournament in the 1900s; in 1910, the term *Five Nations* first appeared.[[160]](#cite_note-SixNations-162) However, the [Home Nations](/wiki/Home_Nations "Home Nations") (England, Ireland, Scotland, and Wales) excluded France in 1931 amid a run of poor results, allegations of professionalism and concerns over on-field violence.[[161]](#cite_note-163) France then rejoined in 1939–1940, though World War II halted proceedings for a further eight years.[[160]](#cite_note-SixNations-162) France has played in all the tournaments since WWII, the first of which was played in 1947.[[160]](#cite_note-SixNations-162) In 2000, Italy became the sixth nation in the contest and Rome's [Stadio Olimpico](/wiki/Stadio_Olimpico "Stadio Olimpico") has replaced [Stadio Flaminio](/wiki/Stadio_Flaminio "Stadio Flaminio") as the venue for their home games since 2013.[[162]](#cite_note-164)

The Rugby Championship is the Southern Hemisphere's annual international series for that region's top national teams. From its inception in 1996 through 2011, it was known as the Tri Nations, as it featured the hemisphere's traditional powers of Australia, New Zealand and South Africa.[[163]](#cite_note-TriNations_History-165) These teams have dominated world rankings in recent years, and many considered the Tri Nations to be the toughest competition in international rugby.[[164]](#cite_note-166)[[165]](#cite_note-167) The Tri Nations was initially played on a home and away basis with the three nations playing each other twice.[[166]](#cite_note-168)

In 2006, a new system was introduced where each nation plays the others three times, though in 2007 and 2011 the teams played each other only twice, as both were World Cup years.[[163]](#cite_note-TriNations_History-165) Since Argentina's strong performances in the 2007 World Cup,[[167]](#cite_note-169) after the 2009 Tri Nations tournament, [SANZAR](/wiki/SANZAAR "SANZAAR") (South Africa, New Zealand and Australian Rugby) invited the [Argentine Rugby Union](/wiki/Argentine_Rugby_Union "Argentine Rugby Union") (UAR) to join an expanded Four Nations tournament in 2012.[[168]](#cite_note-170) The competition has been officially rechristened as The Rugby Championship beginning with the 2012 edition. The competition reverted to the Tri Nations' original home-and-away format, but now involving four teams. In World Cup years, an abbreviated tournament is held in which each team plays the others only once. In 2020, the "Tri Nations" format was temporarily revived due to the withdrawal of South Africa owing to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic").[[169]](#cite_note-Green_light_for_2020_Tri-Nations-171)

### Rugby within multi-sport events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_union&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Rugby within multi-sport events")]

See also: [Rugby union at the Summer Olympics](/wiki/Rugby_union_at_the_Summer_Olympics "Rugby union at the Summer Olympics"), [Rugby sevens at the Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Rugby_sevens_at_the_Commonwealth_Games "Rugby sevens at the Commonwealth Games"), and [Rugby union at the Asian Games](/wiki/Rugby_union_at_the_Asian_Games "Rugby union at the Asian Games")

Rugby union was played at the Olympic Games in [1900](/wiki/Rugby_union_at_the_1900_Summer_Olympics "Rugby union at the 1900 Summer Olympics"), [1908](/wiki/Rugby_union_at_the_1908_Summer_Olympics "Rugby union at the 1908 Summer Olympics"), [1920](/wiki/Rugby_union_at_the_1920_Summer_Olympics "Rugby union at the 1920 Summer Olympics") and [1924](/wiki/Rugby_union_at_the_1924_Summer_Olympics "Rugby union at the 1924 Summer Olympics").[[170]](#cite_note-Rugby_in_the_Olympics-172) As per Olympic rules, the nations of Scotland, Wales and England were not allowed to play separately as they are not sovereign states. In 1900, France won the gold, beating Great Britain 27 points to 8 and defeating Germany 27 points to 17.[[170]](#cite_note-Rugby_in_the_Olympics-172) In 1908, Australia defeated Great Britain, claiming the gold medal, the score being 32 points to three.[[170]](#cite_note-Rugby_in_the_Olympics-172) In 1920, the United States, fielding a team with many players new to the sport of rugby, upset France in a shock win, eight points to zero. In 1924, the United States again defeated France 17 to 3, becoming the only team to win gold twice in the sport.[[170]](#cite_note-Rugby_in_the_Olympics-172)

In 2009, the [International Olympic Committee](/wiki/International_Olympic_Committee "International Olympic Committee") voted with a majority of 81 to 8 that rugby union be reinstated as an Olympic sport in at least the [2016](/wiki/2016_Summer_Olympics "2016 Summer Olympics") and [2020](/wiki/2020_Summer_Olympics "2020 Summer Olympics") games, but in the sevens, 4-day tournament format.[[38]](#cite_note-I.O.C._Decision-40)[[171]](#cite_note-173) This is something the rugby world has aspired to for a long time and [Bernard Lapasset](/wiki/Bernard_Lapasset "Bernard Lapasset"), president of the International Rugby Board, said the Olympic gold medal would be considered to be "the pinnacle of our sport" (Rugby Sevens).[[172]](#cite_note-174)

Rugby sevens has been played at the [Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Commonwealth_Rugby_Sevens_Championships "Commonwealth Rugby Sevens Championships") since the [1998 Games](/wiki/1998_Commonwealth_Games "1998 Commonwealth Games") in Kuala Lumpur.[[173]](#cite_note-175) The most gold medal holders are [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand_national_rugby_sevens_team "New Zealand national rugby sevens team") who have won the competition on four successive occasions until South Africa beat them in 2014.[[174]](#cite_note-176) Rugby union has also been an [Asian Games](/wiki/Asian_Games "Asian Games") event since the [1998 games](/wiki/1998_Asian_Games "1998 Asian Games") in Bangkok, Thailand. In the 1998 and 2002 editions of the games, both the usual fifteen-a-side variety and rugby sevens were played, but from 2006 onwards, only rugby sevens was retained. In 2010, the women's rugby sevens event was introduced. The event is likely to remain a permanent fixture of the Asian Games due to elevation of rugby sevens as an Olympic sport from the 2016 Olympics onwards.

### Women's international rugby

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_union&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Women's international rugby")]

Main article: [Women's international rugby union](/wiki/Women%27s_international_rugby_union "Women's international rugby union")

Women's international rugby union began in 1982, with a match between [France](/wiki/France_women%27s_national_rugby_union_team "France women's national rugby union team") and the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands_women%27s_national_rugby_union_team "Netherlands women's national rugby union team") played in [Utrecht](/wiki/Utrecht_(city) "Utrecht (city)").[[175]](#cite_note-177) As of 2009 over six hundred women's internationals have been played by over forty different nations.[[176]](#cite_note-178)

The first [Women's Rugby World Cup](/wiki/Women%27s_Rugby_World_Cup "Women's Rugby World Cup") was held in Wales [in 1991](/wiki/1991_Women%27s_Rugby_World_Cup "1991 Women's Rugby World Cup") and was won by the [United States](/wiki/United_States_women%27s_national_rugby_union_team "United States women's national rugby union team").[[153]](#cite_note-Women's_Rugby-155) The second tournament took place in [1994](/wiki/1994_Women%27s_Rugby_World_Cup "1994 Women's Rugby World Cup"), and from that time through 2014 was held every four years. The New Zealand Women's team then won four straight World Cups ([1998](/wiki/1998_Women%27s_Rugby_World_Cup "1998 Women's Rugby World Cup"), [2002](/wiki/2002_Women%27s_Rugby_World_Cup "2002 Women's Rugby World Cup"), [2006](/wiki/2006_Women%27s_Rugby_World_Cup "2006 Women's Rugby World Cup"), [2010](/wiki/2010_Women%27s_Rugby_World_Cup "2010 Women's Rugby World Cup"))[[177]](#cite_note-179) before [England](/wiki/England_women%27s_national_rugby_union_team "England women's national rugby union team") won in [2014](/wiki/2014_Women%27s_Rugby_World_Cup "2014 Women's Rugby World Cup"). Following the 2014 event, World Rugby moved the next edition of the event to [2017](/wiki/2017_Women%27s_Rugby_World_Cup "2017 Women's Rugby World Cup"), with a new four-year cycle from that point forward.[[178]](#cite_note-180) New Zealand are the current World Cup holders.

As well as the Women's Rugby World Cup, there are also other regular tournaments (including a [Six Nations](/wiki/Women%27s_Six_Nations_Championship "Women's Six Nations Championship")) run in parallel to the men's competition. The Women's Six Nations, first played in [1996](/wiki/1996_Women%27s_Home_Nations_Championship "1996 Women's Home Nations Championship") has been dominated by England, who have won the tournament on 14 occasions, including a run of seven consecutive wins from [2006](/wiki/2006_Women%27s_Six_Nations_Championship "2006 Women's Six Nations Championship") to [2012](/wiki/2012_Women%27s_Six_Nations_Championship "2012 Women's Six Nations Championship"). However, since then, England have won only in [2017](/wiki/2017_Women%27s_Six_Nations_Championship "2017 Women's Six Nations Championship"); reigning champion France have won in each even-numbered year (2014, 2016, 2018) whilst [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland_women%27s_national_rugby_union_team "Ireland women's national rugby union team") won in 2013 and 2015.

## Variants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_union&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Variants")]

Main articles: [Rugby sevens](/wiki/Rugby_sevens "Rugby sevens"), [Rugby tens](/wiki/Rugby_tens "Rugby tens"), [Touch rugby](/wiki/Touch_rugby "Touch rugby"), [Tag rugby](/wiki/Tag_rugby "Tag rugby"), and [Mini rugby](/wiki/Mini_rugby "Mini rugby")

[![Two teams of players, one in yellow the other in blue, play a form of rugby on a sunlit beach; the central yellow player runs forward, towards the right of picture, clutching the ball with one hand, close to his chest.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Spielszene_BK.jpg/250px-Spielszene_BK.jpg)](/wiki/File:Spielszene_BK.jpg)

[Beach rugby](/wiki/Beach_rugby "Beach rugby") match

Rugby union has spawned several variants of the full-contact, 15-a-side game. The two most common differences in adapted versions are fewer players and reduced player contact.

The oldest variant is [rugby sevens](/wiki/Rugby_sevens "Rugby sevens") (sometimes 7s or VIIs), a fast-paced game which originated in [Melrose](/wiki/Melrose,_Scottish_Borders "Melrose, Scottish Borders"), Scotland in 1883. In rugby sevens, there are only seven players per side, and each half is normally seven minutes. Major tournaments include the [Hong Kong Sevens](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Sevens "Hong Kong Sevens") and [Dubai Sevens](/wiki/Dubai_Sevens "Dubai Sevens"), both held in areas not normally associated with the highest levels of the 15-a-side game.

A more recent variant of the sport is [rugby tens](/wiki/Rugby_tens "Rugby tens") (10s or Xs), a Malaysian invention with ten players per side.[[185]](#cite_note-Completerugby1-189)

[Touch rugby](/wiki/Touch_rugby "Touch rugby"), in which "tackles" are made by simply touching the ball carrier with two hands, is popular both as a training game and more formally as a mixed sex version of the sport played by both children and adults.[[186]](#cite_note-190)[[187]](#cite_note-191)

Several variants have been created to introduce the sport to children with a less physical contact.[[188]](#cite_note-IRB_BGRU_14-192) [Mini rugby](/wiki/Mini_rugby "Mini rugby") is a version aimed at fostering the sport in children.[[189]](#cite_note-Mini_Rugby-193)[[190]](#cite_note-Rutherford1-194) It is played with only eight players and on a smaller pitch.[[189]](#cite_note-Mini_Rugby-193)

[Tag Rugby](/wiki/Tag_Rugby "Tag Rugby") is a version in which the players wear a belt with two tags attached by velcro, the removal of either counting as a 'tackle'. Tag Rugby also varies in that kicking the ball is not allowed.[[191]](#cite_note-195) Similar to Tag Rugby, [American Flag Rugby](/wiki/American_Flag_Rugby "American Flag Rugby"), (AFR), is a mixed gender, non-contact imitation of rugby union designed for American children entering grades K-9.[[192]](#cite_note-196) Both American Flag Rugby and Mini Rugby differ from Tag Rugby in that they introduce more advanced elements of rugby union as the participants age.[[189]](#cite_note-Mini_Rugby-193)

Ten man rugby is a style of play in rugby union in which the team uses the power of its forwards to dominate possession, [scrums](/wiki/Scrum_(rugby) "Scrum (rugby)") and [line-outs](/wiki/Line-out_(rugby_union) "Line-out (rugby union)"), and the kicking ability of its fly-half to attain position. The four [three-quarter backs](/wiki/Three-quarter_back "Three-quarter back") and the full back are not usually used in attack; if they are, this is usually restricted to the inside centre on the [crash ball](/wiki/Crash_ball "Crash ball"), and chasing kicks, especially [garryowens](/wiki/Bomb_(kick) "Bomb (kick)"). Ten man rugby is a very conservative style of play, and not particularly entertaining for the spectator, but can be effective if a team has a strong pack and a good 10, and perhaps less effective backs. The term "ten man rugby" is somewhat derogatory, though most teams will employ these tactics to a degree, especially in foul weather, but usually not exclusively.[[193]](#cite_note-197)[[194]](#cite_note-198)[[195]](#cite_note-199)[[196]](#cite_note-200)

Other less formal variants include [beach rugby](/wiki/Beach_rugby "Beach rugby") and [snow rugby](/wiki/Snow_rugby "Snow rugby").[[188]](#cite_note-IRB_BGRU_14-192)[[197]](#cite_note-201)

## Influence on other sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_union&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Influence on other sports")]

See also: [History of American football](/wiki/History_of_American_football "History of American football"), [Comparison of American football and rugby union](/wiki/Comparison_of_American_football_and_rugby_union "Comparison of American football and rugby union"), [Origins of Australian rules football](/wiki/Origins_of_Australian_rules_football "Origins of Australian rules football"), [Comparison of rugby league and rugby union](/wiki/Comparison_of_rugby_league_and_rugby_union "Comparison of rugby league and rugby union"), [History of rugby league](/wiki/History_of_rugby_league "History of rugby league"), and [Rugby football](/wiki/Rugby_football "Rugby football")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Tom_Wills_1857.jpg/250px-Tom_Wills_1857.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tom_Wills_1857.jpg)

When codifying [Australian rules football](/wiki/Australian_rules_football "Australian rules football") in 1859, [Tom Wills](/wiki/Tom_Wills "Tom Wills") drew inspiration from an early version of rugby he learnt at Rugby School.

[Rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league") was formed after the Northern Union broke from the Rugby Football Union in a disagreement over payment to players. It went on to change its laws and became a [football code](/wiki/Football_code "Football code") in its own right. The two sports continue to influence each other to this day.

[American football](/wiki/American_football "American football")[[198]](#cite_note-Completerugby2-202)[[199]](#cite_note-Stubbs-203) and [Canadian football](/wiki/Canadian_football "Canadian football")[[200]](#cite_note-CanFitba1-204) are derived from early forms of rugby football.[[200]](#cite_note-CanFitba1-204)

[Australian rules football](/wiki/Australian_rules_football "Australian rules football") was influenced by [rugby football](/wiki/Rugby_football "Rugby football") and [other games originating in English public schools](/wiki/English_public_school_football_games "English public school football games").[[201]](#cite_note-205)[[202]](#cite_note-206)[[203]](#cite_note-207)

[James Naismith](/wiki/James_Naismith "James Naismith") took aspects of many sports including rugby to invent [basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball").[[204]](#cite_note-208) The most obvious contribution is the [jump ball](/wiki/Jump_ball "Jump ball")'s similarity to the line-out as well as the underhand shooting style that dominated the early years of the sport. Naismith played rugby at [McGill University](/wiki/McGill_University "McGill University").[[205]](#cite_note-209)

[Swedish football](/wiki/Swedish_football_(code) "Swedish football (code)") was a code whose rules were a mix of [Association](/wiki/Laws_of_the_Game_(association_football) "Laws of the Game (association football)") and [Rugby football rules](/wiki/Rugby_football "Rugby football").[[206]](#cite_note-jönsson203-210)[[207]](#cite_note-211)

Rugby lends its name to [wheelchair rugby](/wiki/Wheelchair_rugby "Wheelchair rugby"), a full-contact sport which contains elements of rugby such as crossing a try line with the ball to score.[[208]](#cite_note-212)

## Statistics and records

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_union&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: Statistics and records")]

See also: [List of top rugby union players by international caps](/wiki/List_of_top_rugby_union_players_by_international_caps "List of top rugby union players by international caps") and [List of top scoring international rugby union players](/wiki/List_of_top_scoring_international_rugby_union_players "List of top scoring international rugby union players")

According to a 2023 report by the Centre for the International Business of Sport, over ten million people play rugby union or one of its variants organised by the IRB.[[209]](#cite_note-Stripes-213) This is a significant increase since the previous report in 2011.[[210]](#cite_note-Global_Rugby-214) The report also claimed that since 2011 participation has grown by 33 per cent in Africa, 28 per cent in South America and 18 per cent in Asia and North America.[[210]](#cite_note-Global_Rugby-214) In 2023 the IRB published a breakdown of the total number of players worldwide by national unions. It recorded a total of 10.9 million players globally, of those, 8.4 million were registered members playing for a club affiliated to their country's union.[[2]](#cite_note-6.6m-2) The 2016 World Rugby Year in Review reported 8.5 million players, of which 3.2 million were registered union players and 1.9 million were registered club players; 22% of all players were female.[[211]](#cite_note-215)

The most capped international player from the [tier 1 nations](/wiki/List_of_international_rugby_union_teams#Tier_1 "List of international rugby union teams") is Welsh captain [Alun Wyn Jones](/wiki/Alun_Wyn_Jones "Alun Wyn Jones") who has played over 150 internationals.
While the top scoring tier 1 international player is New Zealand's [Dan Carter](/wiki/Dan_Carter "Dan Carter"), who has amassed 1442 points during his career.[[212]](#cite_note-216) In April 2010 [Lithuania](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Lithuania "Rugby union in Lithuania") which is a second tier rugby nation, broke the record of consecutive international wins for second tier rugby nations. In 2016, the All Blacks of New Zealand set the new record 18 consecutive test wins among tier 1 rugby nations, bettering their previous consecutive run of 17.[[213]](#cite_note-217) This record was equalled by England on 11 March 2017 with a win over Scotland at Twickenham.[[214]](#cite_note-218) The highest scoring international match between two recognised unions was Hong Kong's 164–13 victory over Singapore on 27 October 1994.[[215]](#cite_note-record_wins-219) While the largest winning margin of 152 points is held by two countries, Japan (a 155–3 win over Chinese Taipei) and Argentina (152–0 over Paraguay) both in 2002.[[215]](#cite_note-record_wins-219)

The record attendance for a rugby union game was set on 15 July 2000 in which New Zealand defeated Australia 39–35 in a Bledisloe Cup game at [Stadium Australia](/wiki/Stadium_Australia "Stadium Australia") in [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney") before 109,874 fans.[[216]](#cite_note-highest_attendance-220) The record attendance for a match in Europe of 104,000 (at the time a world record) was set on 1 March 1975 when [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland_national_rugby_union_team "Scotland national rugby union team") defeated [Wales](/wiki/Wales_national_rugby_union_team "Wales national rugby union team") 12–10 at [Murrayfield](/wiki/Murrayfield_Stadium "Murrayfield Stadium") in [Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh "Edinburgh") during the [1975 Five Nations Championship](/wiki/1975_Five_Nations_Championship "1975 Five Nations Championship").[[216]](#cite_note-highest_attendance-220) This crowd however is an estimate and contemporaneous newspaper accounts list a crowd of 80,000 only. The record attendance for a domestic club match is 99,124, set when [Racing 92](/wiki/Racing_92 "Racing 92") defeated [Toulon](/wiki/RC_Toulonnais "RC Toulonnais") in the [2016 Top 14 final](/wiki/2015%E2%80%9316_Top_14_season#Final "2015–16 Top 14 season") on 24 June at [Camp Nou](/wiki/Camp_Nou "Camp Nou") in [Barcelona](/wiki/Barcelona "Barcelona"). The match had been moved from its normal site of [Stade de France](/wiki/Stade_de_France "Stade de France") near Paris due to scheduling conflicts with France's hosting of [UEFA Euro 2016](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2016 "UEFA Euro 2016").[[217]](#cite_note-221) The record attendance for a regular season club fixture is 83,761, set when [Harlequins](/wiki/Harlequin_F.C. "Harlequin F.C.") played [Saracens](/wiki/Saracens_F.C. "Saracens F.C.") at [Wembley Stadium](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium "Wembley Stadium") during a [Premiership](/wiki/Premiership_Rugby "Premiership Rugby") game in 2012.[[218]](#cite_note-222)

## In culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rugby_union&action=edit&section=50 "Edit section: In culture")]

[![An oil painting of four moustached men, two wearing orange and white striped jerseys and shorts, the other two wearing blue and white striped jerseys and shorts, contesting a rugby ball within an avenue of trees.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d6/Henri_Rousseau_-_The_Football_Players.jpg/250px-Henri_Rousseau_-_The_Football_Players.jpg)](/wiki/File:Henri_Rousseau_-_The_Football_Players.jpg)

Henri Rousseau – *The Football Players* (1908)

Thomas Hughes's 1857 novel *[Tom Brown's Schooldays](/wiki/Tom_Brown%27s_Schooldays "Tom Brown's Schooldays")*, set at [Rugby School](/wiki/Rugby_School "Rugby School"), includes a rugby football match, also portrayed in the 1940s film of the same name. James Joyce mentions Irish team [Bective Rangers](/wiki/Bective_Rangers "Bective Rangers") in several of his works, including *[Ulysses](/wiki/Ulysses_(novel) "Ulysses (novel)")* (1922) and *[Finnegans Wake](/wiki/Finnegans_Wake "Finnegans Wake")* (1939), while his 1916 semi-autobiographical work *[A Portrait of the Artist as a Young Man](/wiki/A_Portrait_of_the_Artist_as_a_Young_Man "A Portrait of the Artist as a Young Man")* has an account of Ireland international [James Magee](/wiki/James_Magee_(sportsman,_born_1872) "James Magee (sportsman, born 1872)").[[219]](#cite_note-223) Sir Arthur Conan Doyle, in his 1924 Sherlock Holmes tale *[The Adventure of the Sussex Vampire](/wiki/The_Adventure_of_the_Sussex_Vampire "The Adventure of the Sussex Vampire")*, mentions that Dr Watson played rugby for Blackheath.[[220]](#cite_note-224)

[Henri Rousseau](/wiki/Henri_Rousseau "Henri Rousseau")'s 1908 work *Joueurs de football* shows two pairs of rugby players competing.[[221]](#cite_note-225) Other French artists to have represented the sport in their works include [Albert Gleizes](/wiki/Albert_Gleizes "Albert Gleizes")' *Les Joueurs de football* (1912), [Robert Delaunay](/wiki/Robert_Delaunay "Robert Delaunay")'s *Football. L'Équipe de Cardiff* (1916) and [André Lhote](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Lhote "André Lhote")'s *Partie de Rugby* (1917).[[222]](#cite_note-226) The [1928 Gold Medal for Art](/wiki/Art_competitions_at_the_1928_Summer_Olympics "Art competitions at the 1928 Summer Olympics") at the Amsterdam Olympics was won by Luxembourg's [Jean Jacoby](/wiki/Jean_Jacoby "Jean Jacoby") for his work *Rugby*.[[223]](#cite_note-227)

In film, Ealing Studios' 1949 comedy *[A Run for Your Money](/wiki/A_Run_for_Your_Money "A Run for Your Money")* and the 1979 BBC Wales television film *[Grand Slam](/wiki/Grand_Slam_(1978_film) "Grand Slam (1978 film)")* both centre on fans attending a match.[[224]](#cite_note-228) Films that explore the sport in more detail include independent production *[Old Scores](/wiki/Old_Scores "Old Scores")* (1991) and *[Forever Strong](/wiki/Forever_Strong "Forever Strong")* (2008). *[Invictus](/wiki/Invictus_(film) "Invictus (film)")* (2009), based on [John Carlin](/wiki/John_Carlin_(journalist) "John Carlin (journalist)")'s book *Playing the Enemy*, explores the events of the [1995 Rugby World Cup](/wiki/1995_Rugby_World_Cup "1995 Rugby World Cup") and [Nelson Mandela](/wiki/Nelson_Mandela "Nelson Mandela")'s attempt to use the sport to connect South Africa's people post-[apartheid](/wiki/Apartheid "Apartheid").[[225]](#cite_note-229)[[226]](#cite_note-230)

In public art and sculpture, there are many works dedicated to the sport. There is a 27 feet (8.2 m) bronze statue of a rugby line-out by pop artist [Gerald Laing](/wiki/Gerald_Laing "Gerald Laing") at [Twickenham](/wiki/Twickenham_Stadium "Twickenham Stadium")[[227]](#cite_note-231) and one of rugby administrator Sir [Tasker Watkins](/wiki/Tasker_Watkins "Tasker Watkins") at the [Millennium Stadium](/wiki/Millennium_Stadium "Millennium Stadium").[[228]](#cite_note-232) Rugby players to have been honoured with statues include [Gareth Edwards](/wiki/Gareth_Edwards_(rugby_union) "Gareth Edwards (rugby union)") in Cardiff and [Danie Craven](/wiki/Danie_Craven "Danie Craven") in Stellenbosch.[[229]](#cite_note-233)

1. **[^](#cite_ref-3)** As of 2014 the International Rugby Board, now known as World Rugby, removed the total breakdown of world-wide player numbers by country, by age and gender to publish instead an overall figure for each nation. This document, titled '119 countries... 6.6 million players' adds the number of registered and unregistered players reported by each country. Some unions only report their registered players, i.e. those who play for an affiliated club or region. Other unions, such as England's [Rugby Football Union](/wiki/Rugby_Football_Union "Rugby Football Union"), also report people taking part in outreach and educational programmes, or unregistered players. In the 2012 figures reported by the RFU they reported 1,990,988 people playing rugby in England, including 1,102,971 under 13s, 731,685 teens and 156,332 seniors. Some of those recorded would have experienced rugby via educational visits to schools, playing tag or touch rugby, rather than playing regularly for a club. The figures released in 2023 give an overall figure of those playing rugby union, or one of its variants, as 10,984,118, but also reports that of that total, 8.4 million are registered players, while 2.5 million are unregistered.
